Summary:
A public elementary school invited guests to demonstrate two Indigenous cultural practices; one was a smudging event held in a classroom and the other was a hoop dance during which the dancer said a prayer. The appellant is the mother of two of the students attending the school. She alleged that the school infringed her Charter guaranteed freedom of religion by compelling her children to participate in religious ceremonies contrary to their own faith and by violating the principle of state neutrality. She sought relief prohibiting the School District from allowing Indigenous cultural events in schools. The trial judge found that the students were observing the events as educational experiences and not participating in them, and that their freedom of religion was not infringed. The trial judge also found that the issues in the litigation were of general public interest and ordered the parties to pay their own costs. The mother appealed the decision about the merits of the complaint and the School District cross appealed on the issue of costs.
Held: Appeal dismissed. Cross appeal allowed. Neither event breached the appellant's freedom of religion or the duty of state neutrality. The trial judge did not make an error in his findings of fact that the children did not participate in the smudging or the prayer and the school did not promote or favour a set of beliefs. The judge considered all the evidence from people present at the events. On the issue of costs, the appellant did not disclose to the trial judge that her complaint was funded by a special interest group, the JCCF. The trial judge's conclusion that Ms. Servatius was of limited means was therefore an irrelevant factor and ought not to have been considered in the exercise of his discretion. The circumstances justify an order that Ms. Servatius pay the costs of the School District: the matter was not a matter of general public interest, but rather, based on facts personal to Ms. Servatius; and the matter was not advanced as an ordinary judicial review proceeding, but rather as litigation over disputed facts. Further, ordinary costs were justified in a situation where Ms. Servatius's petition sought untenable broad prohibitory relief and her funding put her on an uneven footing with the School District.

Reasons for Judgment of the Honourable Justice Griffin:
Introduction
[1] This appeal raises the question of whether a public elementary school infringed the freedom of religion of Ms. Servatius, an evangelical Protestant, when the school hosted two demonstrations of Indigenous cultural practices. One event was a smudging in a classroom; the other was a hoop dance at a school assembly during which the dancer said a prayer. The two Servatius children were enrolled in the school and attended these events which took place during the regular school day.
[2] Ms. Servatius asserted that her religious freedom was violated, alleging that "her children were forced to participate in a religious ceremony" in the school.
[3] There was a factual dispute about the assertion that Ms. Servatius's children were "forced to participate in a religious ceremony", which the judge resolved against Ms. Servatius.
[4] Ms. Servatius also asserted that the school breached its duty of neutrality by facilitating "religious ceremonies" promoting Indigenous spirituality. This too raised factual issues, which the judge resolved against Ms. Servatius.
[5] The judge found there was no interference with Ms. Servatius's or her children's freedom of religion: 2020 BCSC 15 at para. 122. Because of this, he was not required to move on to the second stage of the Charter analysis; namely, if there was interference, determining whether the decisions to host the events reflected a proportionate balance of Charter protections with the statutory mandate of the school employees. The judge dismissed the petition. Subsequently, he ordered each side to bear their own costs: 2020 BCSC 424 [Costs Decision].
[6] Ms. Servatius appeals from the judge's dismissal of her petition. The respondent, the Board of Education of School District No. 70 (Alberni) (the "School District") cross appeals from the judge's costs award.
[7] The Nuu-chah-nulth Tribal Council ("NTC") represents approximately 10,000 members and 14 different First Nations within its traditional territories on the west coast of Vancouver Island. Many Nuu-chah-nulth students attend schools in the School District, including the school where the two events took place. The NTC intervened in this case on invitation by the judge.
[8] The Attorney General of BC appeared as a respondent but took no position with respect to the facts of what occurred at the school. The Attorney General provided evidence and submissions regarding the historical context of the assimilationist policies in Canada and subsequent reforms to the school curriculum in BC to incorporate Indigenous worldviews and knowledge, as well as submissions regarding the applicable statutory framework and legal principles.
[9] As the hearings below and in this Court raise questions about the scope of and compliance with the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, I will start by setting out the nature of this proceeding and the legal framework for considering claims of infringement of religious freedom in the context of the complaint.
[10] I will then review the background facts of what happened, the judge's findings, and the alleged errors on appeal, before turning to my discussion of those alleged errors. I will address the cross appeal on costs last.
Nature of Proceeding
[11] The issues in this case, as in any case, must be analyzed on the basis of the pleadings. Ms. Servatius's allegations about the actions she said violated her religious freedom were set out in her petition, specifically that:
a) the school "imposed prayer and religious rituals" on students, including her children;
b) her daughter was "coerced by her teacher to participate in the Cleansing Ritual", she was "unwillingly subjected to being fanned by smoke", and "she was told that her 'spirit' needed to be 'cleansed' of negative 'energies'"; and,
c) a prayer "based on Aboriginal spirituality" was performed at a school assembly, in which the person performing the prayer made "explicit references to an unspecified 'god'".
[12] Ms. Servatius alleged that these were "religious ceremonies" that her children were "compelled to participate in" against "their conscientious and religious beliefs".
[13] Further, she alleged that "by facilitating religious activities" at the school, the School District violated s. 2(a) of the Charter and breached its duty of religious neutrality.
[14] As relief, Ms. Servatius sought a declaration that the actions of the School District "by imposing prayer and religious rituals on students" at her children's school violated her freedom of religion under the Charter. She also sought an order prohibiting the School District from "facilitating or allowing religious practices" during any time when student attendance was mandatory including, but not limited to, "religious or spiritual rituals, 'cleansings', ceremonies, and prayer". In addition, she sought costs of the proceeding.
[15] Ms. Servatius also gave formal notice of a constitutional question to the province under s. 8(2)(b) of the Constitutional Question Act, R.S.B.C 1996, c. 68. She gave notice of her position that her religious freedom was violated "when her children were force[d] to participate in a religious ceremony" in the school and repeated the relief she sought in the petition.
[16] As a preliminary point, the procedure followed below and on appeal was unorthodox. In the court below, the School District took the position that Ms. Servatius did not properly exhaust other remedies before bringing her petition, and that she should have brought an appeal pursuant to s. 11 of the School Act, R.S.B.C. 1996, c. 412 [School Act]. That section provides for appeals to a school board from certain decisions of school board employees. The judge did not decide the procedural question. The procedural objection was not pursued by the School District on appeal.
[17] Furthermore, Ms. Servatius's petition did not allege that she was seeking judicial review of the school employees' decisions. She alleged detailed facts regarding what happened before and after the events at the school, and how the School District did or did not respond to her complaints. Many of these allegations were disputed by the School District in its response.
[18] For purposes of the appeal, the parties agree that Ms. Servatius's petition was implicitly seeking judicial review pursuant to the Judicial Review Procedure Act, R.S.B.C. 1996, c. 241, in that she sought to review the discretionary decisions of employees of the Ministry of Education, made under the School Act and its regulations, to allow two guests into the school to demonstrate aspects of Indigenous culture.
[19] The parties filed evidence before the judge and sought to have him determine the facts of what happened based on that evidence. As will be seen, at its heart, the appeal is a challenge to the judge's findings of fact. All of this is to say that the structure of the trial court judgment and this judgment on appeal is in response to the parties' chosen framework and the manner in which the case unfolded.
Legal Framework for Consideration of Religious Freedom
Charter Basics
[20] I begin with some basic principles about guaranteed rights and freedoms in Canada.
[21] The Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms is part of the Constitution of Canada, the supreme law of Canada. It applies to the governments in Canada: s. 32. The Charter guarantees certain fundamental freedoms, such as those in s. 2, and rights: i.e., s. 3, democratic rights; s. 6, mobility rights; ss. 7-14, legal rights; s. 15, equality rights; as well as certain French-English language rights.
[22] The guaranteed fundamental freedoms set out in s. 2 are:
2 Everyone has the following fundamental freedoms:
(a) freedom of conscience and religion;
(b) freedom of thought, belief, opinion and expression, including freedom of the press and other media of communication;
(c) freedom of peaceful assembly; and
(d) freedom of association.
[23] The Charter does not attempt to define religion or to prefer one belief over other beliefs that are a matter of conscience, including atheism. The Constitution also protects freedom of conscience, thought, belief, and expression, as well as peaceful assembly and association, all of which could apply to a whole spectrum of secular and spiritual thoughts, beliefs, expressions, and associations.
[24] Section 15 of the Charter guarantees equality rights and protects individuals from discrimination for reasons including their race, national or ethnic origin, colour, religion, sex, age, or mental or physical disability. It provides:
15 (1) Every individual is equal before and under the law and has the right to the equal protection and equal benefit of the law without discrimination and, in particular, without discrimination based on race, national or ethnic origin, colour, religion, sex, age or mental or physical disability.
(2) Subsection (1) does not preclude any law, program or activity that has as its object the amelioration of conditions of disadvantaged individuals or groups including those that are disadvantaged because of race, national or ethnic origin, colour, religion, sex, age or mental or physical disability.
[25] The wide spectrum of beliefs and freedoms protected by the Charter, and the diversity of Canada's population, raises the question: what happens when one person's rights clashes with another's rights, or one person's rights conflicts with what might be necessary for the good of the community, or to protect the rights of many other persons in the community?
[26] These types of inevitable conflicts require an understanding of several points.
[27] First and importantly, the Charter only applies to government actions. It protects the individual against the state.
[28] Second, the approach to Canadian rights and freedoms treats all rights as equally important. The law is clear that there is no hierarchy of rights in the Charter: Dagenais v. Canadian Broadcasting Corp., [1994] 3 S.C.R. 835 at 877, 1994 CanLII 39 (S.C.C).
[29] When state action brings competing Charter rights into conflict, the courts seek a balance to be achieved that respects the importance of both sets of rights: Dagenais at 877; Cambie Surgeries Corporation v. British Columbia (Attorney General), 2022 BCCA 245 at paras. 339-340. As explained in Cambie Surgeries, a single person who complains about their Charter right being infringed does not have a veto over the competing interests of others: at para. 380.
[30] In addition, while the Charter is to be interpreted generously, the rights and freedoms are to be interpreted consistently with the purpose of the protections, or in another way, in light of the interests the Charter was meant to protect: R. v. Big M Drug Mart Ltd., [1985] 1 S.C.R. 295 [Big M] at 344, 1985 CanLII 69 (S.C.C.); Law Society of British Columbia v. Trinity Western University, 2018 SCC 32 [LSBC v. TWU] per Rowe J. at paras. 180-184.
[31] Third, the drafters of the Charter did not intend the enumerated rights and freedoms to be absolute. There will often be a tension between one person's absolute freedom, on the one hand, and, on the other hand, the needs of the community and the rights and freedoms of other people. As an example, in this case it can be seen that there is a tension between Ms. Servatius's claims of religious freedom, on the one hand, and, on the other hand, the freedom of conscience and freedom of expression of the persons who put on the demonstrations in the schools as well as the needs of the community to advance multiculturalism and reconciliation and to teach children tolerance.
[32] The Charter allows governments to limit individual rights and freedoms so long as the government can justify the limits pursuant to s. 1. Section 1 provides:
1. The Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms guarantees the rights and freedoms set out in it subject only to such reasonable limits prescribed by law as can be demonstrably justified in a free and democratic society.
[33] The courts have the role of determining both the scope of the Charter's guaranteed rights and freedoms and whether any government limit is reasonable and justified under s. 1.
[34] When government conduct prescribed by law is found to limit a Charter-guaranteed right or freedom, the courts will employ a s. 1 analysis to determine whether the limit is justifiable in a free and democratic society, and will consider the proportionality of the measure. This way, the approach to Canadian rights and freedoms considers the broader needs of the community, and the rights and freedoms of others.
[35] The Charter also applies to government discretionary decisions, such as decisions by administrative tribunals or government officials acting under a statutory mandate. Where such a decision engages Charter rights, and a party argues that the decision wrongly limited those rights, a different analysis is employed by the courts.
[36] The analysis asks whether, first, the decision limited Charter protections (which may be expressed as Charter values or rights), and if so, whether the decision is reasonable because the decision maker proportionately balanced the Charter protections at play with the decision maker's statutory mandate in the context of the administrative framework and the facts: Doré v. Barreau du Québec, 2012 SCC 12 [Doré] as explained in Loyola High School v. Quebec (Attorney General), 2015 SCC 12 [Loyola]; see also Ktunaxa Nation v. British Columbia (Forests, Lands and Natural Resource Operations), 2017 SCC 54 [Ktunaxa Nation] at para. 136 (dissent judgment); and LSBC v. TWU at paras. 57-59; Redmond v. British Columbia (Forests, Lands, Natural Resource Operations and Rural Development), 2022 BCCA 72 [Redmond], at paras. 47, 55; Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v. Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 at para. 57.
[37] As explained in Loyola, at para. 40, the proportionality analysis is a robust one and "works the same justificatory muscles" as s. 1.
[38] Ms. Servatius is challenging decisions of school employees made pursuant to their mandate under the School Act, and so the Doré/Loyola framework governs.
[39] Other important principles that may affect interpretation of the Charter are set out in ss. 25-28 of the Charter:
25 The guarantee in this Charter of certain rights and freedoms shall not be construed so as to abrogate or derogate from any aboriginal, treaty or other rights or freedoms that pertain to the aboriginal peoples of Canada including
(a) any rights or freedoms that have been recognized by the Royal Proclamation of October 7, 1763; and
(b) any rights or freedoms that now exist by way of land claims agreements or may be so acquired.
26 The guarantee in this Charter of certain rights and freedoms shall not be construed as denying the existence of any other rights or freedoms that exist in Canada.
27 This Charter shall be interpreted in a manner consistent with the preservation and enhancement of the multicultural heritage of Canadians.
28 Notwithstanding anything in this Charter, the rights and freedoms referred to in it are guaranteed equally to male and female persons.
[Emphasis added.]
[40] Sections 25 and 27 add important context to the issues in this case.
[41] No one has argued on appeal that an Aboriginal right is directly implicated in this case, but the parties agree that s. 25 is relevant. The term preferred by the intervener in this case is Indigenous, and that is the term I will use.
UNDRIP
[42] Also relevant to s. 25 of the Charter and the context of this case, is the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples, 13 September 2009, A/RES/61/295 (adopted 2 October 2007) ("UNDRIP").
[43] In BC, the Declaration On The Rights Of Indigenous Peoples Act, S.B.C. 2019, c. 44 [Declaration Act] was passed into law in November 2019. The purpose of the Declaration Act includes affirming the application of UNDRIP to the laws of BC: s. 2(a). The government must take "all measures necessary" to ensure the laws of BC are consistent with UNDRIP: s. 3.
[44] Federally, the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples Act, S.C. 2021, c. 14 [UNDRIPA] received Royal Assent and came into force in June 2021. The UNDRIPA affirmed UNDRIP as a universal international human rights instrument with application in Canadian law: UNDRIPA, s. 4(a). Similarly, it provided that the Government of Canada must "take all measures necessary to ensure" that the laws of Canada are consistent with UNDRIP:UNDRIPA, s. 5.
[45] Article 15 of UNDRIP is relevant to the present appeal as it sets out the right of Indigenous peoples to the dignity and diversity of their culture, traditions, histories and aspirations, as appropriately reflected in education:
Article 15
1. Indigenous peoples have the right to the dignity and diversity of their cultures, traditions, histories and aspirations which shall be appropriately reflected in education and public information.
2. States shall take effective measures, in consultation and cooperation with the indigenous peoples concerned, to combat prejudice and eliminate discrimination and to promote tolerance, understanding and good relations among indigenous peoples and all other segments of society.
[46] The courts in BC have not decided on the extent to which UNDRIP creates substantive rights under s. 25 of the Charter, and it is not necessary to decide it here as the issue is not raised on this appeal.
[47] Nevertheless, as will be seen when I summarize the background facts, BC seeks to incorporate Indigenous culture and perspectives into the public school curriculum, which is consistent with UNDRIP.
Freedom of Religion Pursuant to the Charter
[48] Having touched on the basics of Charter analysis, I will now narrow the focus to briefly touch on the analytical approach to the scope of freedom of religion and claims of infringement.
Scope of Freedom of Religion
[49] There are several decisions of the Supreme Court of Canada which provide guidance on the scope of the Charter guarantee of freedom of religion, including in chronological order: Big M; R. v. Edwards Books and Art Ltd., [1986] 2 S.C.R. 713, 1986 CanLII 12 (S.C.C.) [Edwards Books]; Chamberlain v. Surrey School District No. 36, 2002 SCC 86 [Chamberlain]; Syndicat Northcrest v. Amselem, 2004 SCC 47 [Amselem]; Alberta v. Hutterian Brethren of Wilson Colony, 2009 SCC 37 [Hutterian Brethren]; S.L. v. Commission scolaire des Chênes, 2012 SCC 7 [S.L.]; Loyola; Mouvement laique québécois v. Saguenay City, [2015] 2 S.C.R. 3 [Saguenay]; Ktunaxa Nation; and LSBC v. TWU.
[50] The scope of a Charter-protected right or freedom must start with understanding the purpose of the protection. The purpose of the protection of freedom of conscience and religion was explored in Big M. In summary, it derives from lessons learned in history, where religious allegiances of rulers gave rise to coercive tactics aimed at ensuring conformity to religious beliefs of the ruler and persecution of those who did not share the same beliefs. When power shifted to those of an opposing religious affiliation, the persecuted became the persecutors. Further, people came to oppose the state's interference in belief by recognizing that belief itself was not capable of being compelled, and that state attempts to do so were contrary to an individual's freedom of conscience. Thus, one of the primary purposes of protecting freedom of conscience and religion is to protect people against state coercion, and to leave individuals free to follow their own conscience so long as they do not injure or coerce others. The protection of freedom of conscience and religion also aligns with our modern-day free and democratic political system, which respects the ability of each individual to make free and informed decisions for themselves: see Big M at 344-347.
[51] The judicial exploration of the scope of freedom of religion has revealed two different aspects of the Charter guarantee:
a) It protects people against state interference with their ability to hold and to manifest religious beliefs: Big M at p. 336; LSBC v. TWU at para. 62; Ktunaxa Nation at para. 63.
b) It imposes a duty of neutrality on the state, preventing governments acting to favour any particular religious belief. In this way, it protects non-believers from religion, and protects against religion being promoted by the state: Saguenay.
Sincerity of Belief and Nexus with Religion
[52] The jurisprudence establishes that claimants who seek to show infringement of their Charter guaranteed freedom of religion because of state interference with their religious beliefs must establish two things.
[53] First, the courts will require the claimants to prove the sincerity of their beliefs, and that the beliefs have a nexus with religion.
[54] This requirement was explained in Amselem which involved Jewish owners of residential units who wished to erect "succahs" on commonly owned balconies which enabled them to engage in religious observance during a nine-day religious holiday. The other owners of the building obtained a civil injunction requiring removal of the succahs. The majority of the Court set aside the injunction and found it to infringe religious freedom as guaranteed under the Quebec Charter of Human Rights and Freedoms. It noted that the impairment of the other owners' rights during the limited time of the religious holiday was minimal. The Court's analysis of the scope and content of religious freedom applies equally to the Canadian Charter.
[55] The Court in Amselem emphasized that courts should avoid determining the content of a religious obligation as that would be akin to a religious inquisition. Instead, the role of the courts is to consider the sincerity of a claimant's belief (where sincerity is at issue), to ensure it is in good faith and not fictitious, capricious, or an artifice: at paras. 52, 65; see also Ktunaxa Nation at para. 68; LSBC v. TWU at paras. 63, 70.
[56] This is also described as showing that the claimants sincerely believe that a certain belief or practice is required by their religion or, to put it another way, that their religion calls for a particular line of conduct: Multani v. Commission scolaire Marguerite-Bourgeoys, 2006 SCC 6 [Multani] at para. 35; Amselem at para. 56; see also comments of Rowe J.'s concurring judgment in LSBC v. TWU at paras. 230-234.
State Interference
[57] Second, in order to establish infringement of freedom of religion, the courts require the claimants to show that the state conduct at issue interferes with the claimants' ability to act in accordance with their practice or belief in a manner that is more than trivial or insubstantial. It is not enough for claimants to simply say that their rights have been infringed. Rather, the courts should determine this question by objectively considering the impact of the impugned conduct on the claimants: Ktunaxa Nation at paras. 68, 70; S.L. at paras. 23-24; LSBC v. TWU at para. 74; Amselem at paras. 57-63.
[58] The Supreme Court of Canada has held that a government action that merely recognizes a Charter right or freedom does not, in itself, amount to infringement of someone else's freedom of religion without something more: Reference re Same-Sex Marriage, 2004 SCC 79 at paras. 46, 48.
[59] The requirement that the interference with religious freedom be more than trivial in order to constitute infringement of the Charter guarantee was explained in Edwards Books as follows at 759:
This does not mean, however, that every burden on religious practices is offensive to the constitutional guarantee of freedom of religion. It means only that indirect or unintentional burdens will not be held to be outside the scope of Charter protection on that account alone. Section 2(a) does not require the legislatures to eliminate every minuscule state-imposed cost associated with the practice of religion. Otherwise the Charter would offer protection from innocuous secular legislation such as a taxation act that imposed a modest sales tax extending to all products, including those used in the course of religious worship. In my opinion, it is unnecessary to turn to s. 1 in order to justify legislation of that sort. The purpose of s. 2(a) is to ensure that society does not interfere with profoundly personal beliefs that govern one's perception of oneself, humankind, nature, and, in some cases, a higher or different order of being. These beliefs in turn govern one's conduct and practices. The Constitution shelters individuals and groups only to the extent that religious beliefs or conduct might reasonably or actually be threatened. For a state-imposed cost or burden to be proscribed by s. 2(a) it must be capable of interfering with religious belief or practice. In short, legislative or administrative action which increases the cost of practising or otherwise manifesting religious beliefs is not prohibited if the burden is trivial or insubstantial: see, on this point, R. v. Jones, [1986] 2 S.C.R. 284, per Wilson J. at p. 314.
[Emphasis added.]
[60] Edwards Books cited one case as support for the proposition that an administrative action that imposed only a trivial burden did not infringe religious freedom. The case involved the education of the children of a religious parent, R. v. Jones, [1986] 2 S.C.R. 284, 1986 CanLII 32 (S.C.C.). In Edwards Books, the majority quoted the dissent of Wilson J. on this point.
[61] In Jones, the religious parent, Mr. Jones, believed that God and not the state was the true source of authority over the education of his children. He refused to send his children to public school but, at the same time, did not apply for an available exemption for home schooling as he believed it would be a sin to ask the state to permit him to do God's will. The parent was convicted of truancy. The Supreme Court of Canada upheld the convictions, despite the parent's argument that the laws regarding education, including the laws that he must apply for an exemption for home schooling, interfered with his religious freedom.
[62] The majority in Jones accepted that the laws interfered with Mr. Jones's religious freedom in a minimal way, but also had little difficulty concluding that the interference was justified under s. 1. Indeed, the Court took notice of the state's compelling interest in the education of young people. As stated by La Forest J. at 296-97:
If the appellant has an interest in, and a religious conviction that he must himself provide for the education of his children, it should not be forgotten that the state, too, has an interest in the education of its citizens. Whether one views it from an economic, social, cultural or civic point of view, the education of the young is critically important in our society. From an early period, the provinces have responded to this interest by developing schemes for compulsory education. Education is today a matter of prime concern to government everywhere. Activities in this area account for a very significant part of every provincial budget. Indeed, in modern society, education has far-reaching implications beyond the province, not only at the national, but at the international level. Much of what was said by the Supreme Court of the United States in the following passage in Brown v. Board of Education of Topeka, 347 U.S. 483 (1954) at p. 493, has application here:
Today, education is perhaps the most important function of state and local governments. Compulsory school attendance laws and the great expenditures for education both demonstrate our recognition of the importance of education to our democratic society. It is required in the performance of our most basic public responsibilities, even service in the armed forces. It is the very foundation of good citizenship. Today it is a principal instrument in awakening the child to cultural values, in preparing him for later professional training, and in helping him to adjust normally to his environment. In these days, it is doubtful that any child may reasonably be expected to succeed in life if he is denied the opportunity of an education.
The interest of the province in the education of the young is thus compelling. It should require no further demonstration that it may, in advancing this interest, place reasonable limits on the freedom of those who, like the appellant, believe that they should themselves attend to the education of their children and to do so in conformity with their religious convictions. Section 1 of the Charter allows for this. It provides that the rights and freedoms set out in the Charter are subject "to such reasonable limits prescribed by law as can be demonstrably justified in a free and democratic society".
[Emphasis added.]
[63] Justice Wilson's dissent in Jones held that the statutory requirements imposed only a trivial or insubstantial burden on Mr. Jones and did not infringe his freedom of religion, so it was unnecessary to do a s. 1 analysis. Three other justices agreed with this view.
[64] I pause to note that the jurisprudence does not extensively explore the requirement to show that interference with religious freedom must be more than trivial or insubstantial. However, it does not appear that this requirement means that the party complaining must show a major infringement to trigger a Charter analysis. The seriousness of the infringement or its minor nature becomes most relevant in the proportionality analysis, for example, when considering whether there are any conflicts with other rights: see LSBC v. TWU at paras. 87-90, 100.
State Neutrality
[65] I turn to the second aspect of freedom of religion: the duty of state neutrality. The Charter does not expressly impose a duty of religious neutrality on the state. This duty arises from an evolving interpretation of freedom of conscience and religion: Saguenay at paras. 71-72.
[66] Our understanding of freedom of religion includes separation of church and state, and includes the obligation of the state to remain neutral. This is not because the state has hostility towards religion but, rather, because religion occupies a separate sphere than the state. Religious leaders interfering with or dictating state policy is viewed as undemocratic. In this regard, see Jeroen Temperman, State-Religion Relationships and Human Rights Law: Towards a Right to Religiously Neutral Governance, (Leiden: Martinus Nijhoff, 2010) at 136-37.
[67] State neutrality is assured "when the state neither favours nor hinders any particular religious belief, that is, when it shows respect for all postures towards religion, including that of having no religious beliefs whatsoever, while taking into account the competing constitutional rights of the individuals affected": S.L. at paras. 31-32; Saguenay at para. 71. When the state indicates that it adheres to or favours one set of beliefs, it is creating a "hierarchy of beliefs and casting doubt on the value of those it does not share": Saguenay at para. 73.
[68] The duty of neutrality applies to government institutions, not individuals, and it helps preserve and promote multiculturalism and diversity. It encourages everyone to participate fully in public life regardless of their beliefs: Saguenay at paras. 74-75.
[69] The duty of state neutrality does not mean that the state favours atheism or agnosticism by default. It simply means that the state takes no position on questions of religion. This was explained in Saguenay:
[133] Contrary to the respondents' argument, abstaining does not amount to taking a stand in favour of atheism or agnosticism. The difference, which, although subtle, is important, can be illustrated easily. A practice according to which a municipality's officials, rather than reciting a prayer, solemnly declared that the council's deliberations were based on a denial of God would be just as unacceptable. The state's duty of neutrality would preclude such a position, the effect of which would be to exclude all those who believe in the existence of a deity.
[70] Saguenay involved a city mayor who started each municipal council meeting with a prayer at a microphone. The mayor and other counsellors would stand and make the sign of the cross when the prayer was said. One citizen asked that the mayor cease this practice which he said interfered with his own freedom of conscience and religion. The mayor refused and then the council passed a by-law incorporating a prayer into the beginning of their meetings. The citizen brought a complaint which made its way to the Supreme Court of Canada.
[71] In finding that the citizen's complaint must prevail, Gascon J. noted, at para. 64, that state sponsorship of one religious tradition amounts to discrimination against other such traditions.
[72] Saguenay explains that it is no answer for the state to suggest that its form of expression is merely part of a cultural heritage, if the expression identifies the state with a particular religion: paras. 77-78, 87-88. However, the duty of state neutrality is not breached by every expression of religion in the public sphere. Cultural practices may be intertwined with the religious, and not all cultural expressions will breach the state's duty of neutrality: Saguenay, at paras. 87, 116, 131.
[73] A complainant seeking to prove that a state practice breaches the duty of neutrality must prove two components:
a) the state is professing, adopting or favouring one belief to the exclusion of others; and
b) the exclusion has resulted in interference with the complainant's freedom of conscience and religion.
See Saguenay at paras. 83-85.
[74] The first component requires the court to consider the circumstances to determine the state actor's intention behind the action at issue, and to consider the actual effect of the state's action: Saguenay at para. 88.
[75] The second component returns to the test for showing interference with religious freedom. The complainant must establish two components to make out interference: first, that the complainant's religious belief is sincere and, second, that the state's lack of neutrality has interfered with the individual's ability to act in accordance with their own beliefs in a manner that is more than trivial or insubstantial: Saguenay at paras. 85-86, 88.
[76] This was demonstrated in Saguenay where the complainant was an atheist. The prayer at the start of each council meeting made him feel uncomfortable, and had a "severe exclusionary and isolating impact" on him. The Court found that the circumstances in which the prayer was recited "turned the meetings into a preferential space for people with theistic beliefs": Saguenay at paras. 93, 120-121.
Religious Freedom and Schools
[77] The parties accept that the actions of the School District are subject to the Charter. This is consistent with the case law: Chamberlain; Elementary Teachers Federation of Ontario v. York Region District School Board, 2022 ONCA 476 at paras. 39-41; S.L.; Multani.
[78] The duty of state neutrality, recognized by the Charter guarantee of religious freedom, has also been reinforced provincially in schools by virtue of s. 76 of the School Act, which provides:
76 (1) All schools and Provincial schools must be conducted on strictly secular and non-sectarian principles.
(2) The highest morality must be inculcated, but no religious dogma or creed is to be taught in a school or Provincial school.
[79] At the same time, the School Act also emphasizes the broad values of a healthy, democratic, and pluralistic society, as is set out in the preamble:
WHEREAS it is the goal of a democratic society to ensure that all its members receive an education that enables them to become literate, personally fulfilled and publicly useful, thereby increasing the strength and contributions to the health and stability of that society;
AND WHEREAS the purpose of the British Columbia school system is to enable all learners to become literate, to develop their individual potential and to acquire the knowledge, skills and attitudes needed to contribute to a healthy, democratic and pluralistic society and a prosperous and sustainable economy;
[80] An earlier but similar version of the School Act mandate drew comment from then-Chief Justice McLachlin in Chamberlain:
21 The School Act's emphasis on secularism reflects the fact that Canada is a diverse and multicultural society, bound together by the values of accommodation, tolerance and respect for diversity. These values are reflected in our Constitution's commitment to equality and minority rights, and are explicitly incorporated into the British Columbia public school system by the preamble to the School Act and by the curriculum established by regulation under the Act.
23 The message of the preamble is clear. The British Columbia public school system is open to all children of all cultures and family backgrounds. All are to be valued and respected. The British Columbia public school system therefore reflects the vision of a public school articulated by La Forest J. in [Ross v. New Brunswick School District No. 15, [1996] 2 S.C.R. 171], supra, at para. 42:
A school is a communication centre for a whole range of values and aspirations of a society. In large part, it defines the values that transcend society through the educational medium. The school is an arena for the exchange of ideas and must, therefore, be premised upon principles of tolerance and impartiality so that all persons within the school environment feel equally free to participate. As the Board of Inquiry stated, a school board has a duty to maintain a positive school environment for all persons served by it. [Emphasis added by McLachlin J.]
[81] The values of multiculturalism as emphasized in s. 27 of the Charter often come into play when considering claims of violation of religious freedom. The Supreme Court of Canada has consistently cited the state's interest in ensuring respect for a variety of cultures and beliefs, and tolerance for diversity of views, when finding against parties who seek to extend their particular religious beliefs beyond their personal practices to affect state activity or the rights of others: Chamberlain at para. 21; S.L. at para. 54; Saguenay at para. 120, LSBC v. TWU at para. 103.
[82] The courts have recognized that the values of multiculturalism and diversity must be reflected in school policy and decisions. It is a fact of life that children growing up in a diverse society will be exposed to many differences in beliefs. This exposure to diverse viewpoints is desirable in order to teach children respect and tolerance for others: Chamberlain at paras. 25, 33; S.L. at paras. 39-40.
[83] In Chamberlain, the Court quashed a decision of a school board to disallow three books depicting same-sex families as a kindergarten to grade 1 learning resource. The Court noted that the school board's decision was motivated by concerns that some parents had religious objection to same-sex relationships. Their action violated the principles of secularism and tolerance required by the School Act.
[84] In Chamberlain, the Court noted that a school board could not prefer the religious views of some people to the views of other segments of the community but, rather, "[it] must act in a way that promotes respect and tolerance for all the diverse groups that it represents and serves": para. 25. Further, "[p]arental views, however important, cannot override the imperative placed upon the BC public schools to mirror the diversity of the community and teach tolerance and understanding of difference": para. 33.
[85] There is no question that a law that promotes one faith in schools, such as requiring a daily Christian prayer, offends the Charter guarantee of freedom of religion, and cannot be justified under s. 1: Zylberberg v. Sudbury Board of Education (Director), [1988] O.J. No. 1488, 52 D.L.R. (4th) 577 (Ont. C.A.); Russow v. British Columbia (Attorney General), [1989] B.C.J. No. 611, 62 D.L.R. (4th) 98 (B.C.S.C.); Canadian Civil Liberties Assn. v. Ontario (Minister of Education), [1990] O.J. No. 104, 71 O.R. (2d) 341 at para. 3 (Ont. C.A.).
[86] Conversely, school curriculum that exposed children to a comprehensive review of various religions without any attempt at indoctrination and without giving primacy to any religious faith was found not to infringe religious freedom in S.L.
[87] In S.L., Catholic parents were refused the right to exempt their children from a course called Ethics and Religious Culture ("ERC"). They argued that exposing their children to other religions violated their own religious freedom since their faith required them to pass their religious beliefs on to their children. They argued that exposing the children to other faiths was not neutral but, instead, was a form of relativism.
[88] The Court in S.L. looked at the purpose and goal of the ERC program and found it was not to influence young people's specific beliefs: paras. 34-35. The Court also found that the parents had not proven, objectively, that the program interfered with their ability to pass on the precepts of the Catholic faith to their children.
[89] The Court in S.L. noted that teaching children tolerance means exposing them to diversity:
39 In Chamberlain v. Surrey School District No. 36, 2002 SCC 86, [2002] 4 S.C.R. 710, the Court had an opportunity to consider the cognitive dissonance that may be encountered by children growing up in a diverse society. The Chief Justice made the following comments (paras. 65-66):
Children encounter [some cognitive dissonance] every day in the public school system as members of a diverse student body. They see their classmates, and perhaps also their teachers, eating foods at lunch that they themselves are not permitted to eat, whether because of their parents' religious strictures or because of other moral beliefs. They see their classmates wearing clothing with features or brand labels which their parents have forbidden them to wear. And they see their classmates engaging in behaviour on the playground that their parents have told them not to engage in. The cognitive dissonance that results from such encounters is simply a part of living in a diverse society. It is also a part of growing up. Through such experiences, children come to realize that not all of their values are shared by others.
Exposure to some cognitive dissonance is arguably necessary if children are to be taught what tolerance itself involves.
40 Parents are free to pass their personal beliefs on to their children if they so wish. However, the early exposure of children to realities that differ from those in their immediate family environment is a fact of life in society. The suggestion that exposing children to a variety of religious facts in itself infringes their religious freedom or that of their parents amounts to a rejection of the multicultural reality of Canadian society and ignores the Quebec government's obligations with regard to public education. Although such exposure can be a source of friction, it does not in itself constitute an infringement of s. 2(a) of the Canadian Charter and of s. 3 of the Quebec Charter.
[Emphasis added.]
[90] Similarly, school policy providing that religious programs in schools must not seek to indoctrinate and must not give primacy to any particular religious faith was held not to infringe religious freedom in Bal v. Ontario (Attorney General), [1997] O.J. No. 2597, 151 D.L.R. (4th) 761 (Ont. C.A.).
Proportionality Analysis
[91] As I touched on earlier, when complaining parties prove that a decision by a statutory decision maker limits their Charter rights, the next step in the analysis asks whether the decision at issue is reasonable because it reflects a proportionate balance between the Charter protections at play and the relevant statutory objectives of the decision maker, also known as the Doré/Loyola framework: Redmond at para. 51.
[92] Limits on religious freedom can be justified where acting on those beliefs would cause harm to or interfere with the rights of others, such as the right to equality: Big M at pp. 346-347; Multani at para. 26; LSBC v. TWU at para. 101.
[93] A helpful example of the proportionality approach to justification of a Charter breach can be seen in LSBC v. TWU. There, the Court upheld a decision by the Law Society of British Columbia ("LSBC") to not approve a law school proposed by Trinity Western University ("TWU") that imposed a mandatory religious covenant on all students, whether they were of the same religion or not. The covenant's effect would have excluded and degraded LGBTQ students. The majority of the Court accepted that the decision did impose a limit on freedom of religion, but found that it was justified given the LSBC mandate to uphold and protect the public interest in the administration of justice, which included protecting the rights and freedoms of all parties.
[94] The Court in LSBC v. TWU at para. 95 noted that not approving the school because of the covenant was a minor limit on religious freedom, but approving it would undermine equality of LGBTQ students as they would have fewer opportunities to attend law schools relative to others. In short, the Court found that the LSBC decision reflected a proportionate balance between the Charter protections at play and its statutory mandate.
[95] Applying the concept of proportionality requires consideration not only of the rights of the individual complainant, but also of the broader values of a free, democratic, and pluralistic society, with the understanding that sometimes conflicts between these things are inevitable. The Court in LSBC v. TWU held:
100 The limitation on religious freedom in this case must be understood in light of the reality that conflict between the pursuit of statutory objectives and individual freedoms may be inevitable. minor limits on religious freedom are often an unavoidable reality of a decision-maker's pursuit of its statutory mandate in a multicultural and democratic society.
101 In saying this, we do not dispute that "[d]isagreement and discomfort with the views of others is unavoidable in a free and democratic society" (C.A. reasons, at para. 188), and that a secular state cannot interfere with religious freedom unless it conflicts with or harms overriding public interests (para. 131, citing Loyola, at para. 43). But more is at stake here than simply "disagreement and discomfort" with views that some will find offensive. This Court has held that religious freedom can be limited where an individual's religious beliefs or practices have the effect of "injur[ing] his or her neighbours or their parallel rights to hold and manifest beliefs and opinions of their own" (Big M, at p. 346).
[Emphasis added.]
[96] The concurring judgment of Rowe J. in LSBC v. TWU found that TWU wished to impose the mandatory covenant on students who did not share the faith, so it was coercive of other people and not protected by the scope of freedom of religion. As stated by Rowe J. at para. 251: "a right designed to shield individuals from religious coercion cannot be used as a sword to coerce [conformity to] religious practice".
[97] As noted by the Supreme Court of Canada in LSBC v. TWU at para. 53, not all administrative decision-making requires the same procedure, and any review of the decision must consider the context and nature of the decision-making.
[98] I now turn to the facts of the present case.
Background
[99] The events in question took place in an elementary school in Port Alberni, a small city on the west coast of Vancouver Island. There are approximately 250 students in the school, which contains kindergarten through grade 7. The school is administered by the respondent School District.
[100] Schools administered by the School District are within traditional Nuu-chah-nulth territories. Approximately one-third of the students within the district are Indigenous.
[101] The federal government followed an assimilationist policy towards Canada's Indigenous peoples in the late 19th century, continuing well into the 20th century. In modern times and after much evidence-gathering and study, this policy was identified as cultural genocide by the Truth and Reconciliation Commission: Honouring the Truth, Reconciling for the Future: Summary of the Final Report of the Truth and Reconciliation Commission of Canada (Winnipeg, MB: Truth and Reconciliation Commission of Canada, 2015) at 1 ("TRC").
[102] As part of its assimilationist policy, Canada adopted a residential school program for Indigenous children, separating them from their parents, indoctrinating them in Christianity, and punishing the children harshly if they spoke their own languages and engaged in their own cultural practices. Housed institutionally, without the protection and nurturing of their parents and community, many Indigenous children in these schools were subjected to emotional, physical and sexual abuse, and neglect. Indigenous parents and communities were left grieving and bereft. The last of Canada's residential schools did not close until the 1990s.
[103] The Alberni Indian Residential School operated from 1891-1973, located not far from the public school that is the subject of this case. Other litigation has proven that brutal abuse of Indigenous children took place at this school: Blackwater v. Plint, 2005 SCC 58.
[104] A direct link can be drawn between the residential schools' legacy of harm and the lower success rates of Indigenous children in schools as compared to other students. As the judge in the present case summarized:
[23] The last of the residential schools closed in the 1990s, but the history of Indigenous peoples in British Columbia since European contact makes it easy to understand why Nuu-chah-nulth children have not thrived in the public school system. The TRC found that the residential school system "degraded Aboriginal culture and subjected students to humiliating discipline," and that residential schools "must bear a portion of responsibility for the current gap between the educational success of Aboriginal and non-Aboriginal Canadians" (p. 132). The evidence in our case establishes that a substantial gap continues to exist in the Alberni School District.
[105] In recent years, Canada has begun to reckon with its historical mistreatment of Indigenous people. There has been great pressure on all of Canada's institutions to take concrete steps to address the harms and prejudices of the past, and to forge a mutually respectful path forward. The word reconciliation is sometimes used to encompass this goal, and I use it in this context.
[106] The TRC's action plan called on governments to carry out reconciliation in education by reforming the curriculum to integrate the history and contributions of Indigenous peoples, and to share information about how to best teach these subjects to students: TRC at 7. The TRC Call to Action No. 63(iii) calls for building student capacity for intercultural understanding, empathy, and mutual respect. Further, the Declaration Act integrated UNDRIP and the right of Indigenous people to have their culture and traditions appropriately reflected in education.
[107] As such, it is uncontroversial that public educational institutions need to be involved in reconciliation. Significant efforts have been made to redress the historic legacy of schools being an unsafe place for Indigenous children and to help create better outcomes for Indigenous students.
[108] The School Act provides for the Minister of Education (the "Minister") to make orders concerning educational programs in school: s. 168(2). This includes prescribing the curriculum. The principal of a school is responsible for assisting in carrying out a system of education in accordance with the orders of the Minister; and teachers are responsible for designing, supervising, and assessing educational programs: School Act, ss. 17, 20; School Regulation, B.C. Reg. 64/2021, ss. 4, 5.
[109] Beginning in 2010, the curriculum for kindergarten to grade 12 students was revised and now includes the goals of incorporating Indigenous knowledge and worldviews. Resources published by the Ministry of Education promote learning through experiences, and making schools more comfortable for Indigenous students, which includes providing them with access to their languages and culture.
[110] Teachers have much professional leeway in deciding how to go about teaching the curriculum.
[111] The judge carefully reviewed the history and purpose of the revision to the curriculum to incorporate Indigenous knowledge and worldviews, at paras. 29-33. In summary, he found the curriculum had several purposes:
[29] The purposes of integrating Indigenous knowledge and worldviews into the K to 12 curriculum included recognizing that British Columbia schools serve students from diverse cultures and backgrounds; reflecting the fact that Indigenous knowledge and worldviews are part of the historical and contemporary foundation of British Columbia and Canada; beginning to address misunderstanding of Indigenous cultures; improving school success for Indigenous students; and encouraging mutual understanding and respect amongst all students.
[112] Further, the judge noted the evidence of one classroom teacher who said that this broadened perspective assists Indigenous students and helps them succeed in school as follows:
[33] Through her work as a classroom teacher, and her work with FNESC and other organizations, Ms. Chrona is knowledgeable about development of the new curriculum and about factors that help Indigenous learners succeed. Based on her years of experience working in schools, she endorses the TRC's observation that the relationship between Indigenous peoples and the education system is "at best strained." The increased emphasis on integration of Indigenous knowledge and perspectives into the curriculum was made to move toward an education system that is culturally relevant for both Indigenous and non-Indigenous learners. I accept Ms. Chrona's evidence that educators are coming to understand, as she does, that "one of the elements that helps learners be successful in the K-12 system is that they see themselves reflected in the curriculum," and that "teaching Indigenous culture through experiences which authentically represent that culture in schools and classrooms is a more effective teaching tool than from the study of Indigenous culture only from a textbook." She notes that the number of Indigenous learners obtaining their grade 12 diploma has risen at the same time that Indigenous curriculum content and approaches to education have increased -- although completion rates for Indigenous students both province-wide and in the Alberni School District continue to markedly lag behind the rates for non-Indigenous students.
[113] The NTC enters into agreements with school districts to advocate for culturally relevant teaching and to provide day-to-day support for Indigenous students. The NTC employs education workers who support Indigenous cultural education in schools in the School District. Ms. Sherri Cook is one of those workers, and worked with the school in question.
[114] At a staff meeting just before the start of the school year in September 2015, Ms. Cook raised the possibility of conducting a smudging in classrooms. Three teachers took up the idea, two of whom were the teachers of the Servatius children.
[115] Ms. Cook prepared a letter to be signed by the school principal, to advise parents about the smudging. She did not know exactly what form the demonstration would take place when she wrote the letter.
[116] The principal's letter became an important piece of evidence for Ms. Servatius since it raised the possibility that the event would be both a learning experience and a participatory one. However, the judge found that the smudging did not take place in the way it was described. The full content of the letter is set out in the judge's reasons:
[55] The petitioner argues that the smudging occurred as it was described in the school principal's letter. I find that it did not. However, I think the content of the letter explains, at least in part, the petitioner's strong feelings about the smudging. I have underscored the most problematic aspects of this letter:
A great welcome to 2015/2016 opportunity has been offered to our class. It is a Traditional Nuu-chah-nulth Classroom/Student Cleansing. We will have a guest Nuu-chah-nulth Member, along with our Nuu-chah-nulth Education Worker, Sherri Cook, in our classroom to teach us about Nuu-chah-nulth Culture and History.
Nuu-chah-nulth People believe strongly that "Hii-Shuukish-Tsawalk" (everything is one; all is connected). Everything has a spirit and energy exists beyond the end of school one year and into the next. This will be our opportunity to learn about Nuu-chah-nulth Traditions and experience cleansing of energy from previous students in our classroom, previous energy in our classroom and cleanse our own spirits to allow GREAT new experiences to occur for all of us.
All participants will hold on to cedar branches (each student will feel the bristles of each branch to remind them that they are alive and well to embrace life and all that it offers) and/or "Smudged" (smoke from Sage will be fanned over the body and spirit).
Classroom and furniture will also be cleansed to allow any previous energy from: falls, bad energy, bullying, accidents, sad circumstances, etc. to be released and ensure the room is safe for all and only good things will happen.
If you have any questions about the "Cleansing" or would like to observe please contact Sherri Cook [telephone number and email address].
Thanks for your time and consideration, we look forward to this teaching and experience with your child.
[Emphasis added.]
[117] The judge found that the smudging did not involve the children holding cedar branches nor was smoke waved over the children: para. 56.
[118] The smudging event took place on September 15 and was conducted by an Indigenous woman who was introduced as an Elder. The principal's letter did not arrive at the Servatius home in time for Ms. Servatius to object to her children's attendance at the smudging. When she found out that it had already taken place, she was upset. Mr. and Ms. Servatius wrote to the superintendent of the School District to express their concerns. They stated that they supported their children learning about other cultures and traditions, but they did not agree with "the forced participation in spiritual/religious practices without parents [sic] written consent" and believed "these types of practices do not have a place in the regular classroom curriculum".
[119] Later, in January 2016, the school hosted a hoop dancer who performed at a school assembly at the initiative of the school principal. The principal was taking advantage of province-wide funding for artistic activities in schools. The sponsor of these events provides educators with a catalogue of activities to choose from, and the principal selected this activity from the catalogue.
[120] During his performance, the hoop dancer said a prayer.
[121] Ms. Servatius's children were in the assembly for the hoop dancing and the prayer.
[122] Mr. and Ms. Servatius then wrote to the School District superintendent and board members to complain about the prayer, although they acknowledged that their children found the hoop dancing interesting, impressive, and they "really enjoyed" it. Ms. Servatius asserted that it was a Nuu-Chah-Nulth prayer and part of the Nuu-Chah-Nulth belief system (she was mistaken). She said that, in Canada, school prayer is disallowed based on the Charter. She demanded that the school board provide her with something in writing as to the measures that will be taken to "address this repeated offence" and "written assurance this will not happen again in any of the district schools".
[123] Subsequently, in July 2016, counsel with an organization based in Calgary called the "Justice Centre for Constitutional Freedoms" ("JCCF") wrote to the Chair of the School District's Board of Education and made a number of demands on behalf of Ms. Servatius.
[124] On October 25, 2016, counsel for the School District responded. This letter set out the district's position that the activities that had taken place in the school were permissible. The letter provided assurance that the School District was committed to ensuring best practices in informing parents about cultural performances and processes by which students may opt out of activities that may conflict with their family's beliefs. As well, the School District was committed to ensuring that, when cultural speakers or performers are present within the School District, they are made aware of acceptable communication within the public school system. The latter reference suggests the possibility that the School District would, in the future, inform guest speakers and performers about schools' secular mandate and values of pluralism, but nothing was put in evidence about whether any formal policy was adopted in this regard.
[125] Ms. Servatius was unsatisfied with this response and commenced the petition proceeding against the School District.
Judgment Under Appeal
Dismissal of Petition
[126] The judge took time to carefully review and summarize the historical and social backdrop that led to the decision to modify the BC curriculum to include Indigenous worldviews and perspectives.
[127] He identified and determined the factual issues of what actually happened at the school in respect of the smudging and the hoop dancer's prayer. There were conflicts in the evidence between the Servatius's daughter and her teachers and other adults who were present during these events. The judge rejected the daughter's evidence and preferred the evidence of the other people present at the events.
[128] Factually, the key question was whether the children were "forced to participate" in a "religious ceremony", which was Ms. Servatius's position, or whether they simply were exposed to educational experiences, which she admitted was not objectionable.
[129] The judge found the following facts:
a) The smudging did not take place as the daughter described. Her backpack and her desk were not smudged. Only the classroom's perimeter walls and door frames were smudged, and it only involved a very small amount of smoke. The Elder used an abalone shell and eagle feather. The Elder described the traditions and beliefs associated with smudging and sprinkled in a few words in the Nuu-chah-nulth dialect. No students held the feather or had smoke fanned on to them: paras. 48, 51.
b) "[T]he students' participation was limited to learning: observing, listening, and taking in the smell of the burning sage"; para. 51.
c) The principal's letter did not accurately describe what took place. Students witnessed the smudging demonstration but did not hold cedar branches, and were not smudged or cleansed: para. 55.
d) The Servatius's daughter was not fanned by smoke, was not uncomfortable during the demonstration, and was not told her spirit needed to be cleansed of negative energies: para. 58.
e) The judge preferred the teacher's evidence to that of the Servatius's daughter regarding the opportunity to leave the classroom. The teacher gave students the opportunity to go outside due to the anticipated smoke, but the Servatius daughter took part in the demonstration with enthusiasm and did not ask to leave the classroom: para. 53.
f) The event that took place during the school assembly in January 2016, when the hoop dancer said a prayer, did not result in the students' participation in a spiritual or religious practice: para. 62. The judge rejected the daughter's evidence that she was told to bow her head and close her eyes as an untrue embellishment. Instead, the judge accepted the evidence of teachers present that the students did not participate in the prayer: para. 63.
[130] In addition, the judge rejected Ms. Servatius's evidence that the School District's superintendent told her that "there is more tolerance for Aboriginal religion than your religion". The superintendent denied saying this and the judge accepted his evidence. The judge noted that this was Ms. Servatius's misunderstanding and indeed her essential point in the litigation: she believed that the School District was privileging Indigenous spirituality over her family's beliefs: para. 64.
[131] The judge instructed himself on the law applicable to the s. 2(a) Charter guarantee of religious freedom. He identified the wide scope of freedom of religion as discussed in Big M. He noted the burden on Ms. Servatius to prove infringement as established in the case law reviewed above, including Amselem, S.L. and Saguenay. He noted that Ms. Servatius relied on different routes to an infringement, namely, direct interference in religious freedom as well as a breach of the duty of state neutrality. He set out the content of the duty of state neutrality as explained in S.L. and Saguenay, stating that the duty is fulfilled when the state neither favours nor hinders any particular religious belief: at paras. 66-68. The appellant does not assert that the judge misstated the law.
[132] The judge accepted the concession by the respondent School District that Ms. Servatius's religious beliefs are sincere: para. 75.
[133] The judge recognized that Ms. Servatius held the burden of proving infringement, and that her assertion that the events interfered with her religious freedom required objective proof: paras. 68, 75.
[134] The judge spent some time on the context of the school's decisions to invite Indigenous guests to the school to demonstrate cultural events. He found that "[e]ducators are holding these events to teach about Indigenous culture, and to introduce students to Indigenous perspectives and worldviews": para. 85.
[135] The judge considered the factual question of whether or not the School District professed, adopted, or favoured Indigenous spirituality. He held that, when considered in the full factual context, the two events, the smudging and the hoop dancing demonstrations (i.e., including the prayer said by the hoop dancer), were not an expression of the School District's beliefs or an expression of religious favouritism by design or in their execution: para. 94.
[136] The judge also found that Ms. Servatius failed to prove that her children were compelled to participate in Indigenous spiritual practices or affirm spiritual beliefs associated with these practices. Rather, what occurred were "efforts to teach, in a memorable way, about Indigenous beliefs": paras. 102, 108.
[137] As a result, the judge found that there was no interference with Ms. Servatius's religious freedom.
[138] As such, the judge found it unnecessary to move on to consider the second stage of the analysis, that is, whether the decisions to host the events reflected a proportionate balance of the Charter rights at play with the statutory objectives of the School Act.
Costs Decision
[139] The judge subsequently received written submissions on costs and delivered the Costs Decision.
[140] The ordinary rule is that costs are awarded against the losing party in litigation, which in this case would be Ms. Servatius.
[141] The judge exercised his discretion to order that each side bear their own costs for reasons that I will address when analyzing the cross appeal.
Alleged Errors on Appeal
[142] Ms. Servatius asserts that the judge made errors in concluding that neither the cleansing nor the prayer by the hoop dancer infringed Ms. Servatius's religious freedom. She maintains that there were two ways that these events infringed her religious freedom: one was by compelled participation in a "religious ceremony", interfering with her and her children's ability to act in accordance with their religious beliefs, and the second was by breaching the duty of state neutrality, by promoting "Indigenous spiritual beliefs" over other beliefs.
[143] As framed, Ms. Servatius's success on this appeal depends on showing that the judge made errors in his findings of fact. This is a high burden to meet.
[144] I pause to note that a premise of Ms. Servatius's arguments is that the judge was required to make findings of fact, even if this was a matter of judicial review, because the context was one where no reasons for the administrative decisions were given (nor is it suggested reasons ought to have been given) and the question of whether her Charter rights were engaged required findings of fact.
[145] Appellate courts will not interfere in findings of fact absent the appellant showing that the judge made a "palpable and overriding" error. This means an error that is obvious and that affected the outcome of the case. The standard can be met when the judge misapprehended the evidence or overlooked relevant evidence that would have affected the outcome. However, merely showing that a different conclusion could be reached if the evidence was weighed differently does not meet the standard. The role of appeal courts is not to second-guess the weight given to the evidence: Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33; Nelson (City) v. Mowatt, 2017 SCC 8 at para. 38; and in the context of judicial review, see C.S. v. British Columbia (Workers' Compensation Appeal Tribunal), 2019 BCCA 406 at para. 44.
[146] The cross appeal brought by the School District says the judge erred in his costs award by not considering all of the relevant factors that would justify departure from the normal rule that the losing party pay costs and erred in considering that the litigation raised issues of general public interest.
[147] Since the awarding of costs is a discretionary decision, the School District also has a high burden to meet.
[148] An appeal court will not interfere with a discretionary decision unless it can be established that the court misdirected itself, gave no or insufficient weight to relevant considerations, or came to a decision that is so clearly wrong that it amounts to an injustice: Penner v. Niagara (Regional Police Services Board), 2013 SCC 19 at para. 27; Agar v. Morgan, 2005 BCCA 579.
Discussion
Did the Judge Err in Finding That the Charter Was Not Engaged?
[149] I will address Ms. Servatius's two grounds of appeal in the order she presented them. She argues that the judge erred in not finding there was an infringement of her religious freedom, because the judge erred in not finding that the two events:
a) compelled her children to participate in a "religious ceremony", in conflict with her faith; and
b) promoted Indigenous beliefs above others, violating the duty of state neutrality.
Was There Compelled Participation in a "Religious Ceremony"?
[150] As mentioned, the School District conceded below that Ms. Servatius has a sincere belief that has a nexus with religion: para. 75. In summary, Ms. Servatius's belief is that she and her family cannot accept the cleansing of their spirits by anything other than the blood of Jesus, nor can they participate in religious, spiritual, or "supernatural" rituals or ceremonies that contradict their own faith.
[151] Ms. Servatius says that she does not object to her children learning about others' beliefs, including Indigenous beliefs, so long as they do not participate in an actual religious or spiritual ceremony. However, she characterizes the smudging and the prayer recited by the hoop dancer as imposing a "religious ceremony" on her children contrary to their own faith. She says that, since her children are required by law to attend school, they were compelled to participate in a religious ceremony contrary to their beliefs, which violated her religious freedom as a parent to impart her religious beliefs to her children.
[152] It is clear that the judge properly identified the factual issue before him: whether the evidence established that the children were participating in a ceremony that did not conform with the family's religious beliefs or whether they were merely being taught about another's beliefs or culture. Ms. Servatius concedes that mere education about another's beliefs does not interfere with her religious freedom.
[153] The judge correctly recognized that the question of whether Ms. Servatius's sincere belief was interfered with is to be determined by the judge, not subjectively by Ms. Servatius: paras. 105-108; S.L. at para. 24.
[154] The judge resolved this factual issue against Ms. Servatius. He found that the children's presence at the events in question did not amount to compelled participation in a "religious ceremony" but, rather, amounted to learning about Indigenous practices and culture: paras. 75, 104-108.
[155] In other words, the decisions by the school employees to host the smudging event did not engage the Charter. There was evidence to support the judge's findings.
The Smudging
[156] The school employees who were present during the smudging event in the classroom gave evidence that the children were not participating in the smudging, but that they were actually observing it as part of a lesson about others' beliefs. The adults present gave evidence that the Elder did not take steps to "cleanse" the children, such as blowing smoke over them but, instead, demonstrated what a smudge would look like by waving a small amount of smoke at the walls and doors, all the while explaining what this represented in Indigenous beliefs.
[157] The initial thrust of Ms. Servatius's case was an attempt to prove that her children participated in a smudging ceremony against their will. She believed that they held cedar branches while having sage smoke fanned over their bodies and spirit. She also claimed that her daughter was "unwillingly subjected to being fanned by smoke".
[158] The only piece of evidence of active participation and coercion during the smudging came from Ms. Servatius's daughter who swore an affidavit and was cross-examined about it. She suggested that the Elder who performed the smudging waved smoke over the children's backpacks and on the desks where they sat. She said the Elder told them that the smoke would give them good luck. The daughter's evidence was that there was so much smoke it was getting hard to breathe, that she had difficulty seeing, and that some students were coughing so much they were allowed to leave. The daughter also gave evidence that she told her teacher that she wanted to leave the classroom, but the teacher refused to give her permission.
[159] This evidence as to the nature and extent of the smudging was directly contradicted by others present. The amount of smoke described by the daughter could have set off fire alarms, of which the adults present were very mindful. It also made little sense that the teacher would allow some students to leave the classroom but would not allow Ms. Servatius's daughter to leave.
[160] The judge considered the daughter's evidence. He did not overlook it or make any mistakes about the content of her evidence, but he rejected it and preferred the evidence of the other witnesses who described what happened.
[161] The judge accepted the teacher's evidence that she allowed students to leave if they wanted, but the daughter did not request to do so. It was open to the judge to make these factual findings.
[162] When considerable evidence contradicted the daughter's version of participation in the smudging, Ms. Servatius's focus shifted. She swore a new affidavit to suggest that her children's mere attendance in a classroom, when smudging was being demonstrated, amounted to participation in a "supernatural" or "religious" ceremony which was contrary to her religious beliefs.
[163] On appeal, Ms. Servatius suggests the judge overlooked this argument and erred by failing to draw the inference that the smudging demonstration was in fact a spiritual or "supernatural" experience that affected everyone in the room, so that merely being in the same room and watching it amounted to forced participation in the religious ceremony. She seeks to have this Court draw this inference on the evidence.
[164] I disagree that the judge overlooked the argument that mere presence in the classroom, when smudging of the walls and doors was being demonstrated, amounted to participation in a "religious ceremony" contrary to Ms. Servatius's religious beliefs. The judge expressly considered this argument at paras. 104-107 of his reasons.
[165] Remembering that the assertion that one's religious freedom has been interfered with has to be proven objectively on the facts by looking at the impact of the impugned conduct on the claimants, the judge found this aspect of the complaint was not proven, and that, in the circumstances of this case, more than mere presence was required to show actual interference with Ms. Servatius's children's ability to act in accordance with their religious beliefs: para. 107.
[166] To support her argument on appeal, Ms. Servatius wants us to pick and choose small pieces of evidence that support her version of events, and ignore the broader body of evidence that supports the judge's conclusions. Yet, she has not demonstrated that the judge misunderstood or overlooked material evidence.
