[167] In particular, Ms. Servatius places considerable emphasis on the letter to parents drafted by Ms. Cook and signed by the principal, as well as excerpts of Ms. Cook's evidence, to support her theory that the smudging demonstration was meant to be an actual cleansing ceremony that the children participated in and were subjected to. However, while this evidence establishes that Ms. Cook personally holds beliefs about smudging, it does not correctly relay what actually happened in the classroom.
[168] The judge carefully considered the letter and Ms. Cook's evidence so it cannot be said he overlooked this evidence. He found that what occurred was not as described in the letter: paras. 55-56, 58, 93-94. It was open for him to make this finding.
[169] Ms. Cook's evidence and that of the three teachers who hosted the smudging events in their classrooms supported the judge's finding that the children were not participants in the smudging; rather, they were being taught about Indigenous beliefs about smudging.
[170] Ms. Servatius's lawyer rigorously cross-examined all these witnesses and put to them the theory that the children participated in a "ceremony" or "ritual". Ms. Cook and the three teachers rejected this characterization of the event.
[171] Ms. Cook resisted the characterization of what occurred as a "ritual" and described it as an event that the students could witness. Although she believes in and practices smudging, she did not consider herself to be engaging in a personal practice when these demonstrations were taking place. She said the smudge was not for her; it was for observation. She also explained that from her perspective, her culture is not a religion; it is a way of life. She was adamant that the children were not participating; they were watching a cultural practice. She pointed out that the children did not: hold a feather; hold the smudging bowl; hold the sage; pray; or do "any of the things that would be ceremonial". She explained she would "never force anything upon anybody given my history of my parents going to residential school".
[172] It was put to Ms. Cook in cross-examination that smudging could be taught a different way, but she made the point that there is not much First Nations history in textbooks. There was evidence establishing that the depiction of Indigenous people in textbooks has often been through a European lens which distorted and diminished the Indigenous experience, making it important that Indigenous people be involved in teaching their own history. Ms. Cook did not accept the suggestion that one of the ways to teach this would be to watch videos depicting smudging, and she questioned why one would watch a video when an Elder could come to the class.
[173] In response to questions during cross-examination, one of the teachers, Ms. Iacuzzo, said she did not think that what was happening was an interaction with the "unseen world" or anything "supernatural", and said "spirits" were not mentioned during the demonstration. She considered it simply a demonstration of Indigenous culture, and said it did not feel like a "ceremony". She disagreed that the children participated and described them as "observers".
[174] Another teacher, Ms. Dekoninck, also rejected the word "ceremony" to describe what took place and called it a "demonstration". She thought what happened during the event is not what would occur if it were a real ceremony in Indigenous culture. She did not think it was a real smudging, "worshipping", or "ritual" but, rather, a demonstration for educational purposes.
[175] The daughter's teacher, Ms. Dyer, was also extensively cross-examined by Ms. Servatius's counsel. Counsel put to her that watching a documentary about a ceremony and "experiencing the cleansing power of the ceremony" are "two different things", but the teacher replied that "[a]s it was a demonstration where the students didn't participate, I would actually equate it to watching a documentary on television". She also stated that the letter sent home to parents was "wholly inaccurate", and that she did not hold any personal beliefs about objects carrying energy, but she did have respect for others' beliefs.
[176] Ms. Dyer testified, under cross-examination, that the Elder was quite careful to say that what she was demonstrating was "what our people believe", "our understanding" and "our knowledge", and did not push the idea that this was what students should or could believe at any point. Ms. Dyer denied the proposition that the students "experience[d] the cleansing" and stated, instead, that they were "demonstrated a cleansing" and that "they were not participants in any way".
[177] Ms. Servatius also relies on her reading of the opinion evidence of Pastor Cox regarding the religious doctrine applicable to her beliefs.
[178] Pastor Cox was the Servatius's family pastor, mentor, and Mr. Servatius's employer, and he relied on what the family told him about the facts of what happened at the school. Despite some overreach in his evidence and concerns expressed by the defendants about his objectivity, his opinion as to matters of the Servatius's faith was accepted into evidence. His evidence was clear: Christians holding faiths such as the Servatius family will not be acting contrary to their faith by learning about other expressions of faith, but their faith requires them to refrain from participation in "spiritual or supernatural ceremonies and rituals" that contradict their faith.
[179] The judge correctly summarized Pastor Cox's opinions and considered carefully the content of the evidence, including his evidence that Ms. Servatius's version of Christianity does not discourage learning about other faiths in or out of a classroom: paras. 56, 104-105, 115, 118. It cannot be said that the judge overlooked or misunderstood material evidence.
[180] Pastor Cox offered some opinions about the practice of smudging, but that was not his expertise. Further, it was not for Pastor Cox to determine the disputed facts about what amounted to participation in the smudging. He believed that the children were actively smudged in accordance with the evidence of Ms. Servatius's daughter, but the judge rejected this evidence and noted:
[105] The petitioner deposes that mere presence at this event would be contrary to her family's religious beliefs. However, proof that the smudging interfered with the petitioner's or her children's ability to act in accordance with their religious beliefs requires more than the petitioner's assertion. She must establish the proposition on an objective basis. In his report, Pastor Cox wrote: "For Christians, one does not have to be conducting a ceremony or actively interacting with the components of a ceremony to be participating in it. A Christian would view the act of sitting passively while smoke is wafted over them and their belongings to 'cleanse' negative energy or spirits as participation, because of the Christian's belief in the supernatural." However, I have found as a fact that the smudging was not carried out in this manner: the smoke was not advertently wafted or fanned over the students or their belongings, as described by the petitioner's daughter in her affidavit (which was provided to Pastor Cox).
[Emphasis added.]
[181] On appeal, Ms. Servatius repeats her position that a video demonstrating a smudging would be acceptable, but a demonstration of smudging in the classroom would not, because that would amount to a forced participation in a "religious ceremony".
[182] Ms. Servatius's argument seems to depend on her attributing to the person demonstrating the smudging a belief that she was dealing with "spirits" in the room, and also attributing to her the "supernatural ability" to impart her "spiritual beliefs" to others who are nearby but are not doing anything physically to indicate they are taking part in the ceremony (such as waving the smoke over themselves or saying a prayer).
[183] The person who demonstrated the smudging was not a party to the case and did not provide evidence.
[184] It is not for Ms. Servatius to impose her own beliefs on what an Indigenous person is experiencing when demonstrating a cultural tradition to non-Indigenous people, as correctly pointed out by the NTC.
[185] The majority judgment and the concurring judgment of Rowe J. in LSBC v. TWU illustrate that freedom of religion does not mean that a religious community or person is entitled to extend their personal religious beliefs to limit the rights of other individuals who are not of the same faith.
[186] There was a strong evidentiary basis supporting the conclusion that the event that took place was not, as Ms. Servatius asserts, a "religious ceremony" or something akin to it. Ms. Cook's evidence, as well as that of other Indigenous witnesses, suggested that some members of the Indigenous community make a distinction between cultural events that they will share publicly, as a matter of community building, and spiritual beliefs and practices that they consider to be inherently private. On the judge's findings of fact, the events here would fall into the former category.
[187] Dr. Lorna Williams's evidence was consistent with this. She is a member of the Lil'wat Nation and Professor Emerita at the University of Victoria. She has been involved in Indigenous education in BC for approximately 45 years. She was instrumental in bringing Indigenous perspectives to the education of children and developing curricula to this end. Among other qualifications, she was the First Nations Education Specialist for the Vancouver School Board, Director of Aboriginal Education Enhancement Branch with the BC Ministry of Education, and a Professor of Indigenous Education and Linguistics at the University of Victoria. She provided evidence on the ways curriculum and teaching methods were developed over time to make schools a more welcoming place for Indigenous students with the goal of improving their graduation rates. These methods include building relationships between Indigenous communities and schools and teaching children about Indigenous knowledge and learning.
[188] In Dr. Williams's affidavit, she identified her history of being involved in discussions about developing curricula that includes Indigenous worldviews and perspectives, and how that intersects with the principle of secularism in public schools. She stated:
57. For people trying to describe our practices they will adopt terms from English, such as "ceremonies" or "religion" or "spirituality" that do not adequately capture the understandings in the Indigenous world.
58. From my perspective, the Indigenous practices that could be introduced in public schools under the new curriculum, such as smudging and hoop dances, are public practices for the purpose of community building and individual attention to gratitude and reciprocity. There is a distinction between what Indigenous peoples will share publicly and what we practice in the privacy of our own communities. From my perspective, what we choose to share publicly are not "religious" practices. However, I do find it difficult to speak in terms of "religion" and "spirits" as these ideas evolved in the Western world.
59. For example, smudging is a practice that Indigenous peoples will share publicly. The purpose of smudging is to bring a community of people together. The smoke in the smudging practice is a symbol that reminds us of our connection to our ancestors and the people who will come behind us. It is a practice that acts as reminder to Indigenous peoples.
60. With respect to hoop dances, these are used by Indigenous peoples as a way of telling our stories. Indigenous peoples historically did not have a written language. To assist us in remember and transmitting our stories to our communities, we would use different methods of communication such as dancing, singing, and drumming.
61. From my perspective, Indigenous practices such as smudging and hoop dances are not "religious" practices as that term is understood in the western world.
[Emphasis added.]
[189] Dr. Williams's evidence supports the view that the Indigenous cultural events that occurred in the school could be intended as community building exercises which complied with the principle of state neutrality.
[190] Dr. Judith Sayers also gave evidence. She is a member of the Hupacasath First Nation where she served as elected Chief for fourteen years. This First Nation is part of the larger Nuu-chah-nulth Nation where she serves as the NTC's President. She is an adjunct professor at the Peter Gustavson School of Business and Environmental Studies at the University of Victoria, and has practised law for many years. She provided evidence about the lingering and intergenerational effects of residential schools on Indigenous children and parents which alienate them from the education system and result in lower educational success rates. She referenced the TRC Final Report and its many findings in this regard.
[191] Dr. Sayers explained the NTC's efforts to increase the success rate of Indigenous students, including creating an education team that advocates for these learners within the school system and works on curriculum development to provide culturally relevant teachings among other things.
[192] Dr. Sayers gave evidence that for the Nuu-chah-nulth, culture is a way of life and a reflection of worldviews and beliefs and not a religion. For example, it would not be possible to convert someone to Nuu-chah-nulth culture.
[193] It was Dr. Sayers' opinion that demonstrating smudging in schools shows Nuu-chah-nulth children that their culture is welcome in schools. It was her opinion that true reconciliation requires a respect for the humanity and culture of First Nations. Her evidence was that many Nuu-chah-nulth spiritual practices are private and not shared in public.
[194] When cross-examined by counsel for Ms. Servatius, Dr. Sayers explained that smudging can have many different purposes. She said she would not describe smudging the way it was described in the principal's letter. She also agreed that smudging is entered into by consent, and she did not know of any situation where people would be smudged against their will.
[195] In summary, there was no suggestion in the evidence that the smudging demonstration was part of a belief system that sought to convert someone from one religion to the religion of the person smudging or to involve an unwilling observer by making them a participant. There was no suggestion in the evidence that anyone involved in these events had a goal to proselytize or to impose their spiritual beliefs or practices on others present, or that they believed it was possible or right to try to do so.
[196] In short, there was simply no objective evidence to support the inference that merely watching the smudging demonstration and learning about its relevance to Indigenous culture amounts to some kind of involuntary participation in a ceremony akin to a "religious ceremony".
[197] Ms. Servatius admitted in cross-examination that having someone else express their beliefs does not impact her own beliefs. Ms. Servatius was free to discuss her children's reaction to the demonstration with them and how her own religious views might contradict the Indigenous beliefs.
The Hoop Dancer's Prayer
[198] Again, context is important when turning to the prayer said by the hoop dancer. The hoop dancer was there to perform an Indigenous art form: hoop dancing. His performance was enjoyed by many, including Ms. Servatius's children. Ms. Servatius's objection was to the prayer.
[199] There is a paucity of evidence about the prayer. Ms. Servatius did not lead evidence about the content of the prayer, or how long it was or even how its recitation unfolded in the hoop dancer's performance despite the fact that her counsel cross-examined teaching staff who were present during the performance. Some present did not even remember it which suggests it was quite brief. The limited evidence about the content of the prayer suggests that the hoop dancer's prayer was giving thanks and mentioned the word "god".
[200] The only evidence that the prayer was coercive in some way came from Ms. Servatius's daughter who testified that children were told to participate by bowing their heads and closing her eyes. However, her evidence was contradicted by other witnesses and rejected by the judge. The judge held:
[62] I am satisfied that what occurred at the January 2016 school assembly did not result in the participation of students in a spiritual or religious practice. The petitioner's daughter describes the prayer as follows:
After the dance, [a First Nations] man used the microphone to say a prayer to a 'god'. I was surprised because I thought it was strange to have a prayer at school assemblies. I was also confused and upset because I did not know what the man was praying to. It felt strange and wrong to be there while the man prayed to his god.
In her viva voce evidence, she described the dance performance as "really good until the end when they made me participate in a form of praying, as they called it." She testified that the dancer said he would be saying a prayer and that he wanted everyone to participate. She testified that she was told -- she did not say by whom -- to bow her head and close her eyes or she would be in trouble. This evidence that the dancer wanted the students to participate and that she was directed to bow her head and close her eyes was not included in her affidavit. I find that these details are embellishments, and I reject them as untrue.
[63] Other witnesses gave evidence about the hoop dancing and prayer. The JHES principal, Ms. Manson, was responsible for inviting the dancer, Teddy Anderson, to the school. Ms. Manson deposes that the students observed the dancer say an Indigenous prayer during the performance, but nothing in the performance encouraged the children to believe in or adopt the culture or religion of the performer; at no time did the students participate in the prayer. Teachers Dekoninck and Iacuzzo were present at the hoop dance, and they also depose that the students did not participate in the dancer's prayer. I accept this body of evidence.
[Emphasis added.]
[201] That meant the only factual question in dispute with respect to the prayer based on the allegations advanced by Ms. Servatius, was whether mere presence in the school assembly when the hoop dancer said the prayer, amounted to participation in a "religious ceremony" as opposed to learning about someone else's beliefs. (I will come back to the breach of state neutrality argument.)
[202] Ms. Servatius's argument that the prayer interfered with her own faith in a coercive way was based on the notion that the children were "forced" to participate in the prayer, and not that they were merely present when it was recited. The daughter conceded that she would not be upset by the hoop dancer's prayer if he said it, and she just sat and watched it. In her evidence, she admitted to the following:
Q Would you have objected if you weren't told to bow your head or close your eyes?
A Objected meaning?
Q Well, would you be upset that the hoop dancer said a prayer if you just sat and watched?
A No.
Q That would be okay?
A Yes.
[203] As the judge correctly noted, S.L. supports the conclusion that religious freedom is not compromised when students are taught about other beliefs. This is also consistent with the evidence of Ms. Serviatus's own beliefs, as well as the opinion evidence of her pastor.
[204] After considering all of the evidence, the judge concluded that mere presence at the smudging demonstration and the prayer by the hoop dancer did not interfere with Ms. Servatius's religious beliefs but, rather, were efforts to teach children about Indigenous beliefs:
[107] I conclude that proof on an objective basis of interference with the ability of the petitioner or her children to act in accordance with their religious beliefs requires more than the children being in the presence of an Elder demonstrating a custom with spiritual overtones or being in the presence of a dancer who said a brief prayer .
[108] I am not satisfied, on an objective standard, that being in a classroom containing smoke produced by an Elder setting sage alight, or being in the presence of a dancer saying a prayer, interferes with the ability to act in accordance with religious beliefs. To my mind, these are admirable and admissible efforts to teach, in a memorable way, about Indigenous beliefs. It is surely proper, and advisable in light of the historical circumstances, that the School District organize such events. Being taught about beliefs is not an infringement of religious freedom -- even when this teaching is done by an Elder at close range and in a manner that engages a student's sense of smell as well as her senses of sight and sound, and even if this teaching results in some "cognitive dissonance."
[Emphasis added.]
[205] The judge's findings are supported by the evidence and he did not make a palpable and overriding error.
[206] Ms. Servatius has not established any ground for appellate interference in the judge's conclusions that the Servatius children were not forced to participate in any "religious ceremony", and that Ms. Servatius's Charter rights were not limited by the two events that took place. As such, the judge was correct to dismiss the complaint that her freedom of religion was engaged and infringed by the decisions of school staff to allow guests into the school to demonstrate the two events.
Was There a Breach of the Duty of Neutrality?
[207] In her petition, Ms. Servatius asserted that the School District breached its duty of religious neutrality by "facilitating religious activities" at the school which interfered with her religious freedom. She advanced this ground as distinct from the argument that her religious beliefs were interfered with because her children were forced to participate in a "religious ceremony".
[208] Importantly, the thrust of Ms. Servatius's complaint about breach of the duty of state neutrality was that the state was favouring Indigenous spirituality over other religious beliefs, by hosting the smudging event and by allowing the hoop dancer to say a prayer as part of his performance.
[209] Ms. Servatius's position in this case seems to suggest that there is a bright line division between cultural practices, on the one hand, and "religious" or "spiritual" practices on the other. She seems to suggest that the smudging demonstration crossed the line from teaching about a cultural practice into a "religious" or "spiritual" practice if the Elder demonstrating the smudging had "spiritual beliefs" about the smudging. Likewise, Ms. Servatius seems to suggest that any prayer is forbidden in a secular public setting because it crosses the bright line from a cultural practice to a "religious" or "spiritual" practice regardless of the context.
[210] Respectfully, this approach is too simplistic. It not only fails to recognize the complexity of cultural traditions and spiritual or religious beliefs, but also fails to recognize that alleged breaches of Charter rights are necessarily informed by the factual context.
[211] If we know anything about traditions around the world, there is no bright line between culture and religion but, rather, there is a spectrum along which some practices are purely cultural, some are purely religious, and many have an amalgamation of both. For some people, culture and religion are tightly interwoven strands that affect much if not all of their daily activities. It would be easy to point to an event and suggest it has religious overtones for some members of a faith but not for others. For example, statutory holidays in Canada recognize certain important events in the Christian calendar that are often celebrated culturally by Christians and non-Christians alike, even if the same events may also have a religious meaning for Christians.
[212] Furthermore, the approach to determining rights under Canada's Charter is nuanced and not as absolutist as Ms. Servatius's bright line position suggests. The Canadian approach to rights analysis under the Charter requires the courts to take the full factual context into account. This approach also recognizes that tolerance for the rights of others and respect for a diversity of viewpoints is a lens through which we must determine the scope of rights.
[213] Where the line is between the state hosting a cultural event and professing religious favouritism will depend on the facts. As noted in Saguenay, the key is to determine if the practice identifies the state with a religion:
[87] This being said, it must be recognized that the Canadian cultural landscape includes many traditional and heritage practices that are religious in nature. Although it is clear that not all of these cultural expressions are in breach of the state's duty of neutrality, there is also no doubt that the state may not consciously make a profession of faith or act so as to adopt or favour one religious view at the expense of all others .
[88] Thus, it is essential to review the circumstances carefully. If they reveal an intention to profess, adopt or favour one belief to the exclusion of all others, and if the practice at issue interferes with the freedom of conscience and religion of one or more individuals, it must be concluded that the state has breached its duty of religious neutrality. This is true regardless of whether the practice has a traditional character.
[214] Ms. Servatius argues that the smudging was a "religious" activity at least in the broad sense of a spiritual "ritual", even if it only involved cleansing parts of the classroom and not the students.
[215] In assessing this allegation, the judge had to objectively determine the school's purpose in hosting the presentations and the actual effect of the presentations, including whether they infringed freedom of religion even if that was not their purpose: S.L. at paras. 24, 27, 34-35; see also Edwards Books at paras. 61, 81-82, 96. The judge assessed this allegation accordingly.
[216] As I have already reviewed, the judge did not accept Ms. Servatius's subjective views and characterization of what occurred in the smudging presentation as a "religious activity", even in the broad sense. Ms. Servatius relied on the principal's letter to parents, but the judge rejected the notion that this letter accurately described the events that actually took place: para. 93.
[217] Equally, the judge did not accept that the subjective intentions of some of the people involved in hosting the events determined whether or not the events demonstrated religious favouritism: paras. 98-100.
[218] The judge was thorough and careful in analysing the full context of the smudging and hoop dance presentations at the school. He held that the purpose of these events was not to profess or favour Indigenous beliefs but, rather, to teach students about Indigenous culture and to introduce them to Indigenous perspectives and worldviews as consistent with the curriculum: para. 85. This finding is supported by the evidence.
[219] The judge correctly noted that the context of these events was important. He carefully reviewed the history of government efforts to destroy Indigenous culture, which included placing Indigenous children in abusive school environments, and noted that one purpose of the curriculum was to make schools safe for Indigenous students. He found that the purpose of the events in question served the goal of making schools a culturally safe space for Indigenous students to learn by reflecting and respecting their culture. He held:
[86] In oral argument, counsel for the petitioner sought to support the idea that there is a critical distinction between a field trip and a classroom experience on the basis that the classroom is a special place, and that classroom walls are there to "keep the students safe." This submission highlights why context matters so much in this case, and is an illustration of why it is important to heed the caution in Saguenay that the state's duty of neutrality must be interpreted "not only in a manner consistent with the protective objectives of the Canadian Charter, but also with a view to promoting and enhancing diversity" (para. 74). Recall the evidence that one legacy of the residential schools policy is that intergenerational survivors associate schools and education with abuse and trauma. To help address this lingering impact, the NTC have set out to make schools in Nuu-chah-nulth territories places where Nuu-chah-nulth students "could see themselves and their culture reflected, to make the schools a culturally safe space." By way of the curriculum redesign, the Ministry of Education has strongly endorsed this line of thinking, and it is evident that the School District supports this goal. One practical way to help make schools culturally safe spaces is to have students learn -- in their classrooms -- from Indigenous people. On the evidence, I am satisfied that the classroom is rendered a safer place by having a Nuu-chah-nulth Elder visit the classroom, show students how a smudging is conducted, and explain the significance of smudging in Nuu-chah-nulth culture.
[Emphasis added.]
[220] After considering the relevant evidence, the judge rejected the argument that the smudging and hoop dance events showed favouritism toward Indigenous spirituality in their effect. Instead, he found that they reflected a desire to incorporate the teaching of Indigenous worldviews and perspectives into the curriculum: para. 94.
[221] It was open to the judge, on the evidence, to find that the objectives of the school in hosting these Indigenous presentations, and the ways these presentations were carried out, were religiously neutral. There was evidence to support the conclusions that the school did not set out to promote one set of spiritual or religious beliefs over another and did not, in fact, show favouritism towards Indigenous beliefs.
[222] The determination that the smudging was not a "religious ceremony", contrary to Ms. Servatius's assertions, was a question of fact and the judge's conclusion was supported by ample evidence.
[223] Turning separately to the prayer said by the hoop dancer, it is clear that a prayer is religious in character. Even so, that is not the only factor in determining whether the school breached the duty of neutrality.
[224] The judge found objectively that the purpose of the school officials in inviting the hoop dancer to perform was religiously neutral: it was to incorporate the teaching of Indigenous culture into the curriculum in a way that would engage the students. The judge also recognized that even a religiously neutral purpose does not insulate a government actor if in effect what occurs turns out not to be religiously neutral: paras. 97-100.
[225] The question is whether the effect of hosting the hoop dancer's performance crossed the line of state neutrality when the dancer incorporated a prayer into his performance.
[226] I accept the general argument that reciting a prayer at a government-sponsored event can implicate the freedom of conscience of those who do not hold the same beliefs as the person praying. I also appreciate that reciting a prayer in a government institution may cause considerable discomfort to those who do not hold the same religious beliefs as the person praying. They may face an internal dilemma about whether to leave the venue and risk unintentionally conveying disrespect for the person praying, or to stay at the risk of offending their own conscience by implying that they accept and hold the same beliefs. They may also feel as though they are excluded and outside the sphere of influence of the people who allowed the prayer to take place and who hold the same beliefs as the person praying. These concerns can be amplified when the persons attending the event had no notice that it would involve a prayer but simply had it imposed on them.
[227] However, we cannot simply assume that the prayer said by the hoop dancer during his hoop dance performance created any of these problems in the absence of evidence. Other than the evidence of the Servatius's daughter which the judge did not accept, there was no evidence from anyone present that these conflicts of conscience arose.
[228] Several circumstances in this case suggest that the school did not adopt or favour the Indigenous belief system associated with the prayer: (1) it was not recited by anyone with authority in the school; (2) it was recited by a guest who was there to perform a hoop dance ceremony to introduce Indigenous culture to students; (3) the prayer seems to have been incidental to the guest's hoop dance performance; (4) there is no evidence that school officials took part in the public display of praying or encouraged students to do so or took any step to encourage students to hold the same beliefs; and (5) there is no evidence that the guest knew the students or had any influence over any of the students' lives in or out of the school.
[229] As mentioned, the content and length of the prayer is not known, but nothing suggests that the dancer was trying to promote one set of religious beliefs over another. The dancer was not Indigenous although his promotional materials describe the prayer as an "Indigenous prayer". It is naïve to suggest that the Elder who demonstrated the smudging holds the same views as the hoop dancer. Expecting all Indigenous people or admirers of Indigenous cultures to believe in the same thing is comparable to assuming that all theists belong to the same belief system. There is no evidentiary basis for saying that the fact both events took place in the school at different times proves the school was showing favouritism for one belief system.
[230] The facts of this case are very different from the circumstance where a school official, whether an employee or volunteer, actively shows a preference for a particular faith by engaging in displays of praying at school events, thereby creating an impression that others who hold the same beliefs will be favoured and those who do not will be out of favour. It is also very different from a situation where the school regularly invites guests to say a prayer at school events.
[231] This case was not at all akin to the facts in Saguenay, where the Court found that the regular recitation of a prayer at council meetings was profession of a faith by the state:
[118] These comments confirm that the recitation of the prayer at the council's meetings was above all else a use by the council of public powers to manifest and profess one religion to the exclusion of all others. It was much more than the simple expression of a cultural tradition. It was a practice by which the state actively, and with full knowledge of what it was doing, professed a theistic faith. What the respondents are defending is not a tradition, but the municipality's right to manifest its own faith. A sure sign of this is the respondents' statement in this Court that the appellants were attempting to prevent the municipality [TRANSLATION] "from expressing its belief" (R.F., at para. 116). In my opinion, nothing could conflict more with the state's duty of neutrality. Tradition cannot be used to justify such a use of public powers.
[119] I repeat that what is at issue here is the state's adherence, through its officials acting in the performance of their functions, to a religious belief. The state, I should point out, does not have a freedom to believe or to manifest a belief; compliance with its duty of neutrality does not entail a reconciliation of rights
[Emphasis added.]
[232] It was the state officials' public steps showing preference for a faith that turned the public space in Saguenay into a preferential space for people holding the same faith: para. 120.
[233] Unlike the situation in Saguenay, in this case, the one-off prayer said by a school guest as part of his performance of a hoop dance did not amount to the school showing a preference for a faith or creating a preferential space for anyone, Indigenous or otherwise.
[234] Ms. Servatius asks this Court to interpret S.L. as standing for the proposition that, in order for the state to demonstrate that it was acting in a neutral way, the state needed to show that it had a comprehensive course touching on various religions, and that, otherwise, any demonstrations of Indigenous practices would violate neutrality. Respectfully, this is not a correct reading of S.L.
[235] The question in S.L. was whether a course touching on various religious and ethical points of view violated the freedom of religion of Catholic parents who wished to raise their children in the Catholic faith alone. In finding that the course did not violate freedom of religion, the Court did not establish a rule that would preclude a school from taking steps to host displays of Indigenous culture, even where the person displaying the cultural practice might hold spiritual beliefs connected to the practice. Each case has to be decided on its own facts.
[236] Here, the judge properly instructed himself on the law, referring to S.L. and Saguenay. In this case, the duty of state neutrality turned on the factual question of whether the school professed favouritism for a set of beliefs. The judge found that the school did not do so:
[93] The issue of whether the School District professed, adopted, or favoured Indigenous spirituality turns on the facts. The petitioner argues that the School District's purpose in arranging the smudging is disclosed in the JHES principal's letter, and the letter reflects an attitude of favouritism toward and profession of Indigenous spirituality. I think the focus of this argument is too narrow. The whole context of why the School District sponsors such events must be examined, and a misstatement of the School District's intentions by an individual educator is not determinative of the purpose behind the events under examination. In this connection, it is important to remember that it is not the principal's letter but the smudging and the hoop dancer's prayer that are alleged to have infringed the Charter.
[94] I conclude that these smudging and hoop dancing demonstrations were in no way -- either by design or in their execution -- an expression of the School District's beliefs or an expression of religious favouritism. Rather, the organization of these events reflected a gathering momentum to incorporate the teaching of Indigenous worldviews and perspectives. I accept the evidence of Ms. Dyer, the petitioner's daughter's teacher, that the smudging or anything that the Elder said could not be construed as trying to make someone else believe what the Elder believed -- when the Elder spoke, she used words like "our tradition is" or "we believe." And, arranging for students to observe hoop dancing, even if the dancing is accompanied by an Indigenous prayer, cannot reasonably be interpreted as the School District professing, adopting, or promoting religious beliefs.
[95] The S.L. case establishes that the duty of state neutrality is met "when the state neither favours nor hinders any particular religious belief" (para. 32). As Gascon J. said in Saguenay, at para. 74, what is required is neutral public space free from coercion, pressure, and judgment on the part of public authorities in matters of spirituality, but not the homogenization of this space. The exhortation of Gascon J. to locate the boundaries of the state's duty of neutrality not only in a manner consistent with the protective objectives of the Charter, but also with a view to promoting and enhancing diversity is an important instruction in the matter at hand. The facts in this case do not permit any reasonable conclusion other than that the School District and its representatives neither favoured nor hindered any particular religious belief, and that the JHES events did not violate the state's duty of neutrality.
[Emphasis added.]
[237] Further, to establish that the state infringed the freedom of religion by breaching its duty of neutrality, the complainant must also show that there was actual interference with the person's religion: Saguenay at paras. 85-86, 120-121. In Saguenay, the Court found that the effect of the regular prayer practice at municipal meetings was exclusionary, isolating, and stigmatizing on the complainant:
[120] The prayer recited by the municipal council in breach of the state's duty of neutrality resulted in a distinction, exclusion and preference based on religion -- that is, based on Mr. Simoneau's atheism -- which, in combination with the circumstances in which the prayer was recited, turned the meetings into a preferential space for people with theistic beliefs. The latter could participate in municipal democracy in an environment favourable to the expression of their beliefs. Although non-believers could also participate, the price for doing so was isolation, exclusion and stigmatization. This impaired Mr. Simoneau's right to exercise his freedom of conscience and religion.
[121] In this regard, the evidence accepted by the Tribunal shows that when Mr. Simoneau went to meetings of the municipal council, he had to choose between remaining in the chamber and conforming to the City's religious practice, excluding himself from the practice by refusing to participate in it, and physically excluding himself from the chamber for the duration of the prayer. If he chose to conform to the council's practice, he would be acting in direct contradiction with his atheistic beliefs. If he chose to exclude himself from the prayer either by refusing to participate in it or by leaving the chamber, he would be forced to reveal that he is a non-believer (para. 266). According to the Tribunal's findings, Mr. Simoneau had experienced a strong feeling of isolation and exclusion (paras. 265-66). This led the Tribunal to conclude that the interference caused by this situation was more than trivial or insubstantial (para. 262). Such interference constitutes an infringement of the complainant's freedom of conscience and religion.
[238] Unlike in Saguenay, here, there was evidence to support the judge's conclusion that the hoop dancer's reciting a prayer did not interfere with the Servatius family's freedom of religion.
[239] The focus of Ms. Servatius's evidence, and her argument in the case before the judge, was about the smudging, which she viewed as a "religious ceremony". There was very little focus on the prayer as a stand alone "wrong". Pastor Cox also did not address this in his written opinion or his oral evidence. While Ms. Servatius asserted that her children felt uncomfortable about the prayer, this hearsay evidence was in the context of the assertion that they were forced to participate in it. I refer again to the daughter's evidence that she would not be upset by the prayer if it was just recited in her presence, and she was allowed to just sit and watch it. This is what happened on the facts found by the judge.
[240] Ms. Servatius has not demonstrated that the judge overlooked or misapprehended the evidence. She simply wishes this Court to re-weigh it. That is not this Court's role.
[241] Simply put, it was open to the judge on the evidence to reject the argument by Ms. Servatius that the prayer interfered with the Servatius's family religion.
[242] In these circumstances, Ms. Servatius has not shown that the judge made a palpable and overriding error in concluding, as a matter of fact, that the school did not violate the duty of state neutrality.
[243] It follows that I would dismiss the appeal.
Proportionality Analysis
[244] As touched on earlier when describing the legal framework for this appeal, if there were an infringement of Ms. Servatius's religious freedom, the next question is whether the decisions to host the events in the school reflected a proportionate balance between Charter rights and values engaged by the decisions, and the objectives of the School Act. The proportionality analysis in this case would need to also consider the freedom of conscience and expression of the people who made the presentations, the goals of reconciliation with Indigenous people and the values of pluralism.
[245] Given my conclusions that the two events did not limit Ms. Servatius's Charter rights, it is not necessary to engage in the proportionality analysis.
Did the Judge Err in Not Awarding Costs Against Ms. Servatius?
[246] The respondent School District appeals from the judge's order that each side bear its own costs. In summary, it takes the position that the judge ought to have followed the ordinary rule that the losing party, Ms. Servatius, pay the costs of the winning party, the School District.
[247] The respondent, the Attorney General, takes no position on costs, other than stating none should be awarded for or against it. The NTC does not seek costs.
[248] Ms. Servatius asserts that the judge made no error in the Costs Decision. In her petition pleading in the court below, Ms. Servatius sought costs of the proceeding. However, after the judge decided the matter against her, he invited submissions on costs.
[249] Ms. Servatius argued before the judge that the litigation served the public interest which justified an order that each party bear their own costs. Ms. Servatius also made written submissions to the judge that her family was of "limited means" and would "suffer hardship" if costs were awarded against her, arguing that the School District had a "superior capacity" to pay costs. The judge appeared to accept these submissions.
[250] The judge noted that litigation that involves the public interest may justify departing from the ordinary rule, citing Guide Outfitters Association v. British Columbia (Information and Privacy Commissioner), 2005 BCCA 368 [Guide Outfitters], and MacDonald v. University of British Columbia, 2004 BCSC 412. He stated that the overarching question was whether the usual rule was unsuitable on the facts of the case: Costs Decision at para. 3.
[251] In Guide Outfitters this Court set out the following principles on discretion regarding costs in public interest litigation:
[8] Several judgments of the courts in the province have recognized that questions of whether the public interest is served by the litigation may guide the court in exercising its discretion regarding costs. In [MacDonald v. University of British Columbia, 2004 BCSC 412] at para. 13, Mr. Justice Bauman referred to factors the Ontario Law Reform Commission considered may lead a judge to rule the parties should bear their own costs:
(a) The proceeding involves issues the importance of which extends beyond the immediate interests of the parties involved.
(b) The person has no personal, proprietary or pecuniary interest in the outcome of the proceeding, or, if he or she has an interest, it clearly does not justify the proceeding economically.
(c) The issues have not been previously determined by a court in a proceeding against the same defendant.
(d) The defendant has a clearly superior capacity to bear the costs of the proceeding.
(e) The plaintiff has not engaged in vexatious, frivolous or abusive conduct.
Although I consider these factors as useful ones to guide the Court in the exercise of its discretion as to costs, the overarching question is still whether the normal rule is unsuitable on the facts of this case. As Smith J. (as he then was) said in Sierra Club of Western Canada v. British Columbia (Chief Forester) (1994), 94 B.C.L.R. (2d) 331 at paras. 49-50, 117 D.L.R. (4th) 395 (S.C.), aff'd (1995), 7 B.C.L.R. (3d) 375, 60 B.C.A.C. 230:
I do not think it would be wise to establish a principle that any person bringing a proceeding out of a bona fide concern to vindicate his or her perception of the public interest should be insulated from an award of costs in all cases. Such a motive will always be a relevant and important factor, but it should not be considered to the exclusion of all other relevant and important factors. The Court must retain the flexibility to do justice in each case.
[Emphasis added.]
[252] The judge's analysis and determination of the issue was as follows:
[4] The petitioner has the burden of showing that this is not a suitable case to apply the normal costs rule. Although the case ultimately turned on findings of fact, the litigation raised issues of general public interest including the public interest served by the resolution of issues that might otherwise constrain demonstrations of Indigenous culture and spirituality in schools. The petitioner has not engaged in any vexatious, frivolous or abusive conduct. I am mindful that while the petitioner's means may be limited, the resources available to the School District are scarce. This is a close call, but on balance, considering all of the circumstances, I think this is an appropriate case to exercise my discretion to order that each litigant bear their own costs.
[Emphasis added.]
[253] On appeal, Ms. Servatius initially continued to seek costs of the litigation in her favour, but also argued that it was public interest litigation.
[254] After the hearing of the appeal, this Court invited Ms. Servatius and the School District to file additional written submissions addressing the question of costs in light of their position that the general nature of the proceeding was one of judicial review of an administrative decision maker.
[255] Ms. Servatius then filed a submission taking the position that, because the matter was one of judicial review, costs should not be awarded for or against any party, citing for this proposition Crook v. British Columbia (Director of Child, Family and Community Service), 2020 BCCA 192, Lang v. British Columbia (Superintendent of Motor Vehicles), 2005 BCCA 244.
[256] In her post-appeal written costs submission, Ms. Servatius disclosed, for the first time, that the JCCF was funding her fees and disbursements in the litigation, as well as agreeing to help her pay any award of costs by agreeing to fundraise for her if costs were awarded against her. Although vaguely described, there is no reason to believe that the JCCF, a frequent litigant, does not have the resources to make good on its promise and so this was in effect an agreement by the JCCF to use its resources to indemnify Ms. Servatius for any award of costs against her.
[257] I note that different counsel represented Ms. Servatius at trial than on appeal, although both were supported by the JCCF funding. It would have been preferable for Ms. Servatius to be transparent to the judge about the JCCF funding, as it is clearly relevant to the public interest analysis that a special interest group is funding the litigation, not the named petitioner. In my view, had the judge known this fact he would not have exercised his costs discretion in the way he did. He was clearly influenced by the misleading assertions about Ms. Servatius's capability of weathering the burden of paying a costs award.
[258] This belated disclosure requires considering the costs discretion anew.
[259] In my view, in this case the public interest exception does not justify a departure from the ordinary rule that costs are awarded to the successful party against the unsuccessful party. The case was largely a dispute about facts which were personal to Ms. Servatius and her family and which were disputed by the School District.
[260] Regarding the smudging, Ms. Servatius made it plain that, from her perspective, this was about a single day of smudging demonstrations that took place in one school and involved her children. Indeed, Ms. Servatius's counsel cross-examined several witnesses to lead evidence that proved that the witnesses were not aware of smudging happening on any other occasion in their school, and they were not aware of other schools implementing smudging.
[261] The principal's evidence was that, when the hoop dancer was invited to perform a hoop dance, she did not realize that he would incorporate a prayer into his performance. Whether this was an oversight on her part is not the point: there was no suggestion that schools in the district regularly invite speakers who say a prayer to students.
[262] In short, this complaint concerns two moments of time in Ms. Servatius's children's school years and no indication of any ongoing School District policy or practice that would interfere with her religious beliefs. No novel legal issue was raised but, rather, the application of well-established legal principles to disputed facts. Neither do the issues convincingly extend beyond the particular facts of this case or resolve a legal issue of broad importance which would otherwise not be resolved: Guide Outfitters at para. 8; Victoria (City) v. Adams, 2009 BCCA 563 at para. 189. For these reasons, I do not see this as an appropriate case to depart from the ordinary costs rule.
[263] That takes me to the issue of whether the general principle against awarding costs on judicial review applies in the present case.
[264] The School District acknowledges that there is a general rule, subject to some exceptions that do not apply here, that costs will not be awarded against an administrative tribunal on judicial review, and this has been extended to include a general rule against awarding costs to the tribunal, as held in Lang and Crook. The School District submits that, had Ms. Servatius followed a correct procedural route and appealed the school employees' decisions to the school board pursuant to s. 11 of the School Act, the resultant decision may qualify as quasi-judicial, giving rise to the general rule against awarding costs of a subsequent judicial review. However, the School District submits that this general rule ought not to apply here because the decisions at issue were not quasi-judicial.
[265] Ms. Servatius refers to A.A.A.M. v. Director of Adoption, 2017 BCSC 87 and other decisions of the trial court for the proposition that the general rule against awarding costs on judicial review applies to all challenges including challenges to decisions by administrative decision makers.
[266] In my view, I need not decide the question of whether the general principle against awarding costs on judicial review, as stated in Lang and Crook, ought to be restricted to quasi-judicial decisions as opposed to other decisions by administrative decision makers. This is an interesting issue that likely will be addressed by this Court in the future: see Gonzales Hill Preservation Society v. Victoria (City) Board of Variance, 2022 BCCA 384.
[267] In my view, if there is a general rule against awarding costs in most judicial review proceedings, it would not be determinative here. Regardless of any general rule, costs awards are a matter of discretion and the authorities make it clear that there are exceptions even in judicial review proceedings.
[268] The manner in which this case was framed and conducted, as well as the role of the JCCF, takes this case outside the traditional judicial review mold.
[269] Ms. Servatius did not plead that this was a case seeking judicial review, and the case was not conducted as though it was reviewing a decision based on a record before a decision maker; it was conducted as though two parties disputed the facts of what happened and the judge was required to adjudicate that dispute.
[270] Based on the allegations advanced by Ms. Servatius, the judge was required to weigh disputed evidence and make findings of fact, much like a traditional trial. Eleven affidavits were filed in response to the petition and in response to Ms. Servatius's evidence. Her evidence included her own affidavits and those of her daughter and Pastor Cox. Eleven witnesses were cross-examined by Ms. Servatius's counsel.
[271] In addition, the general rule against awarding costs against the petitioner who loses on a judicial review proceeding is based on policy reasons that do not apply here. Because it would be contrary to policy to award costs against an administrative tribunal that is merely performing its statutory function, it is seen as inconsistent to award costs in favour of the administrative tribunal against a party that is merely challenging the lawfulness of the tribunal's decision affecting that party's interests: 18320 Holdings Inc. v. Thibeau, 2014 BCCA 494 at para. 59.
[272] I do not see that this reasoning applies in a case like the present, where as I have already described, the matter proceeded more as a dispute over facts alleged by the petitioner. Indeed Ms. Servatius sought costs at both levels until changing her position when questioned about it by this Court.
[273] Added to the mix is the fact that the JCCF was effectively insulating Ms. Servatius from the costs of the litigation and did not disclose this to the judge, while her legal counsel (affiliated with the JCCF) was advancing the submission that the public interest exception should apply because Ms. Servatius did not have the means to fund costs.
[274] I agree with the sentiment expressed in Canadian Council for Refugees v. Canada (Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship), 2017 FC 1131 that in litigation purporting to advance public interest arguments "[i]t is generally not appropriate for 'ghost' parties to lurk in the background, providing extensive funding, evidence, advice, or information."
[275] One of the traditional concerns of public interest standing law is to avoid "officious intermeddling", where a party (sometimes referred to in the authorities as a "busybody") without a genuine interest in the subject of the legal complaint stirs up controversy and foments an action of marginal merit. While the law concerning public interest standing has been somewhat relaxed, the awarding of costs continues to serve the important purpose of deterring suits of little merit that are advanced by parties with no real stake in the litigation: see discussion in British Columbia (Attorney General) v. Council of Canadians with Disabilities, 2022 SCC 27 [Council for Canadians with Disabilities] at paras. 42-47, 51.
[276] Therefore, where as here, a party is seeking to avoid the ordinary costs rule on the basis that the litigation is public interest litigation and on the basis that the named party cannot afford costs, it is necessary for the courts to know who is truly financing that party's lawsuit and who is truly at risk for the potential costs award.
[277] Ms. Servatius's petition sought not only a declaration that her religious freedom was violated, but she also sought an order prohibiting the school from "facilitating or allowing religious practices". Ms. Servatius advanced the position before the judge that, by hosting two rather innocuous Indigenous cultural events, the school was favouring Indigenous spirituality, and, as a consequence, no such Indigenous cultural events should ever be hosted in schools. This very broad and vague relief seeking to restrict unknown future actions of the School District from ever hosting Indigenous cultural events was not supported by any version of the facts or interpretation of the law and was untenable from the start. However, its broad implications had to be defended by the School District.
[278] At the hearing of this appeal, now with new counsel (albeit still supported by the JCCF), Ms. Servatius modified her position to claim that Indigenous cultural events could be permitted in schools, so long as parents were given notice and students were permitted to opt out. One wonders about the practical benefits of this litigation given this belated concession. Early on and in response to Ms. Servatius's initial complaints, the School District made an offer to her, through its counsel, to ensure best practices in informing parents about cultural performances and the processes by which students may opt out.
[279] This background highlights the importance of transparency as to who is the real party funding the litigation when a party is seeking to be relieved of paying ordinary costs on the basis of the public interest exception. It must be kept in mind that ordinary costs rules act as a deterrent to advancing untenable positions, overstating the available relief in litigation, and wasting judicial resources on minor complaints that would not usually justify a lawsuit.
[280] In the circumstances, the JCCF's involvement in effect insulated Ms. Servatius from normal costs consequences and put the School District on an uneven playing field in having to defend the very broad relief advanced in this litigation.
[281] Considering all of the above factors, I would set aside the judge's costs award and substitute the ordinary rule: the unsuccessful party, Ms. Servatius, must pay the costs of the School District in the proceeding below and on appeal.
Conclusion
[282] I would dismiss Ms. Servatius's appeal from the judge's conclusion that the school interfered with her freedom of religion.
[283] I would allow the cross appeal from the judge's conclusion that each side bear their own costs. I would order that Ms. Servatius pay the costs of the School District in this Court as well as in the court below.
